name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage of the horse blog that shows featured and latest articles by category, provides a prominent site-wide search entry point, quick navigation to key sections, and a footer newsletter subscription form.

core_features: Load homepage content using getHomepageContent to display featured articles and the latest articles grouped by category.
Provide a site-wide search form that optionally shows live search suggestions using searchArticles and routes users to search_results.html with the appropriate URL parameters.
Expose quick navigation to Training, Health, Competitions, Gear, Reading list, Favorites, Gallery, Contact, and About using URL parameters where required.
Show per-article status indicators for reading list and favorites (read-only) based on data returned by getHomepageContent.
Provide a footer newsletter subscription form that uses createNewsletterSubscription to create new newsletter subscriptions with user-selected topics, frequency, and email format.
user_workflows:
Browse featured and latest articles:
1. User opens index.html.
2. Page initializes and calls getHomepageContent with no parameters.
3. The featured articles list is populated from response.featured_articles.
4. The latest-by-category lists are populated from response.latest_by_category for each category.
5. User clicks an article card or title, triggering navigation to article_detail.html?articleId={Article.id}.
6. User can return via browser back or header 'Home' navigation to re-load the homepage (which calls getHomepageContent again).

Search for an article from the homepage:
1. User focuses the search input in the homepage search form.
2. As the user types, the search form optionally calls searchArticles with parameters: query={currentInput}, category={selectedCategoryOrEmpty}, page=1, pageSize={configuredSuggestionPageSize} to fetch suggestion results.
3. Search suggestions are shown beneath the search field; clicking a suggestion navigates directly to article_detail.html?articleId={Article.id}.
4. When the user submits the search form (pressing Enter or clicking the search button), the page does not call searchArticles; instead, it navigates to search_results.html with URL parameters: query={inputValue}, category={selectedCategoryOrEmpty}, page=1, pageSize={defaultSearchPageSize}.
5. The search_results.html page is responsible for displaying full search results using its own logic.

Navigate to category article lists from the homepage:
1. User sees category quick links or header navigation items for Training, Health, Competitions, and Gear.
2. User clicks a category link.
3. The click triggers navigation to articles.html?categoryId={categoryEnumValueFromLink}.
4. articles.html handles listing and filtering per category.

Access Reading list, Favorites, Gallery, and Contact from the homepage:
1. User clicks 'Reading list' in the header; browser navigates to reading_list.html.
2. User clicks 'Favorites' in the header; browser navigates to favorites.html.
3. User clicks 'Photos' in the header or a gallery teaser; browser navigates to gallery.html.
4. User clicks 'Contact' in the header or a contact call-to-action; browser navigates to contact.html.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the homepage footer:
1. User scrolls to the footer newsletter form.
2. User fills in name and email, selects topic checkboxes, chooses a frequency option and an email format option.
3. User submits the form; the page calls createNewsletterSubscription with parameters mapped from the form fields.
4. On success, the component displays a success message using the returned message and may reset the form fields.
5. On failure, the component displays an error message and keeps the current form values to allow corrections.
interactions: On page load, a data-loader component calls getHomepageContent to fetch featured articles and latest-by-category data; UI sections subscribe to this data and render when it is available.
Clicking a featured or latest article card (title, thumbnail, or dedicated 'Read' interaction) navigates the browser to article_detail.html?articleId={Article.id} without modifying any backend state.
Clicking a category label or dedicated 'More in this category' link on a category section navigates to articles.html?categoryId={Article.category} for that category.
In the search form, typing in the search input updates internal search state; optional debounced calls to searchArticles are triggered when the input is non-empty, updating the suggestions list.
Clicking a search suggestion item navigates directly to article_detail.html?articleId={Article.id} and closes the suggestion list.

Submitting the search form (button click or Enter key) prevents default form submission and instead constructs a navigation URL search_results.html?query={encodedQuery}&category={encodedCategoryOrEmpty}&page=1&pageSize={defaultSearchPageSize}.
Clicking 'Reading list' in the header navigates to reading_list.html.
Clicking 'Favorites' in the header navigates to favorites.html.
Clicking 'Photos' in the header navigates to gallery.html.
Clicking 'Contact' in the header navigates to contact.html.
Clicking 'About' in the footer navigates to about.html.

Submitting the newsletter form in the footer triggers createNewsletterSubscription; success and error states are reflected by showing feedback messages bound to the API response, without reloading the page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not consume any incoming URL parameters (incoming_params is empty), so its behavior is not altered by query strings or hash fragments. All state relevant to this page is managed in memory for the duration of the visit and reset on reload. The primary use of URL parameters is for navigation to other pages:
- Article detail: clicking an article card constructs article_detail.html?articleId={Article.id}. The articleId parameter is taken directly from the Article.id field returned by getHomepageContent.
- Category list: clicking a category link constructs articles.html?categoryId={categoryEnumValue}, where categoryEnumValue is one of the Article.category values for Training, Health, Competitions, or Gear.
- Search results: submitting the search form constructs search_results.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}&category={encodedCategoryOrEmpty}&page=1&pageSize={defaultSearchPageSize}. The category parameter is empty or omitted if no category filter is selected in the search form.
- Other pages: Reading list, Favorites, Gallery, Contact, and About are accessed via direct navigation without parameters.
Internal UI state includes: loading and error flags for getHomepageContent, the current search query text, optional selected search category, current search suggestions and their loading state (backed by searchArticles with page=1 and a configured suggestion page size), and the newsletter form fields plus submission status (pending/success/error) bound to createNewsletterSubscription. None of this internal state is written to or read from URL parameters or localStorage; it is only held in memory while the user remains on the Home page.

components.functionality: On initial page load, fetches unified homepage data using getHomepageContent and exposes it to child components such as featured articles and latest-by-category sections. Manages loading and error states for the homepage content.

Displays a list of featured articles returned from homepage-data-provider. Each item shows article title, category name, tags, optional thumbnail, published date, reading time if available, and read-only indicators for reading list and favorite status.

For each category entry in latest_by_category, renders a category block listing the most recent articles for that category. Includes a category heading and a 'View all' interaction that navigates to the full category listing on articles.html.
Provides quick-access navigation items to the articles.html page filtered by Training, Health, Competitions, and Gear categories. Category identifiers correspond to the Article.category enum values.

Provides a site-wide search input and optional category filter. On submit, navigates to search_results.html with query, category, page, and pageSize URL parameters. During input, it can trigger live search suggestions using searchArticles.

Displays live search suggestions based on the current query in the homepage-search-form. It receives results from searchArticles calls and lists matching article titles (and optionally categories). Selecting a suggestion navigates directly to the chosen article's detail page.

Handles main site navigation: Home, Training, Health, Competitions, Gear, Reading list, Favorites, Photos, and Contact. Uses URL navigation (with categoryId where applicable) without calling additional interfaces.

Provides a form in the global footer for visitors to subscribe to the newsletter. Collects name, email, topic preferences (training tips, competition updates, merchandise offers), preferred frequency, and email format. On submit, calls createNewsletterSubscription and shows success or error feedback based on the response.

Provides footer-level navigation to About and potentially back to Home. Clicking 'About' navigates to about.html; clicking 'Home' (if present) navigates to index.html without re-calling any interfaces beyond the standard homepage load.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a two-column layout: a narrow, fixed-width left rail for the global header navigation (logo + primary nav) and a wide, airy main content canvas on the right. The left column stacks the ornate green plaque logo at the top with navigation items directly below in a single vertical list. The right column begins with a slim header band defined by a thin horizontal rule aligned roughly to the bottom of the logo. Immediately below this band, the homepage search form anchors the main content. Under the search, category quick links provide secondary navigation, followed by a vertically stacked sequence of featured articles and latest articles by category. The footer newsletter and footer navigation links sit at the bottom of the main content column. The data provider is non-visual and simply wraps/feeds these components.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a two-column layout: a narrow, fixed-width left rail for the global header navigation (logo + primary nav) and a wide, airy main content canvas on the right. The left column stacks the ornate green plaque logo at the top with navigation items directly below in a single vertical list. The right column begins with a slim header band defined by a thin horizontal rule aligned roughly to the bottom of the logo. Immediately below this band, the homepage search form anchors the main content. Under the search, category quick links provide secondary navigation, followed by a vertically stacked sequence of featured articles and latest articles by category. The footer newsletter and footer navigation links sit at the bottom of the main content column. The data provider is non-visual and simply wraps/feeds these components.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Horizontally, the left navigation column occupies about 18–22% of the viewport width (e.g., ~260–300px on a typical desktop), while the right main content area takes the remaining 78–82%. Vertically, the logo and primary navigation occupy the upper half of the left column, leaving intentional whitespace below. In the right column, the thin horizontal rule sits just below the logo’s bottom edge, with 16–24px of vertical separation, creating a minimal top band. The search form occupies roughly 60–70% of the main content width, left-aligned. Category quick links, featured articles, and latest-by-category sections stack vertically with 32–48px gaps between them. The footer newsletter and footer navigation share the lower portion of the right column, with generous top margin so they feel separated from the article content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is kept deliberately low. Navigation items in the left rail have around 8–12px vertical padding and 0–16px horizontal padding, plus 8–16px between items, making the list airy and easy to scan. In the main content area, each block (search, quick links, featured articles, latest-by-category, newsletter, footer links) has generous vertical spacing (32–48px) so the page reads like a calm editorial layout rather than a dense portal. Within article lists, individual items are separated by at least 16–24px and never feel crowded. The search suggestions dropdown is lightweight, with comfortable line spacing and breathing room around results.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the green plaque logo at the top-left. From there, it moves downward through the vertically stacked navigation items in the left rail. The subtle caret beside the expander item (such as 'OUR RIDES') provides a small secondary attraction within that column. The thin horizontal rule at the top of the right column then pulls the eye horizontally into the main canvas. The search form just below that rule becomes the primary focal point for interaction, followed by a downward scan: quick category links, then the featured articles section, then the latest articles by category. Finally, as the user scrolls, the eye arrives at the footer newsletter form and then the lighter-weight footer navigation links, closing the visual journey. Throughout, whitespace between sections reinforces a slow, contemplative reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The homepage presents as a quiet, editorial-style canvas with all functional elements anchored in the upper-left quadrant. A narrow, fixed left sidebar holds the ornate green plaque logo at the very top, with a vertically stacked list of all-caps navigation links paced evenly down the column, one item showing a small caret to indicate nested content. To the right, a thin horizontal rule runs from just beyond the logo’s right edge across the full width of the main content area, establishing a subtle top boundary. Below this rule, the right-hand column opens into a wide, mostly white space. The search bar sits near the top-left of this canvas as a confident yet understated entry point, followed by a thin band of category quick links. Further down, the featured articles section occupies the upper half of the content region, with a few spaciously separated article cards, and the latest-by-category section continues the vertical stacking below it. The bottom of the main column houses a centered newsletter subscription form and a small cluster of footer navigation links, both surrounded by generous whitespace. The left rail feels fixed and steady while the right column scrolls, giving the whole page an airy, personal-blog feel where navigation and content are clearly separated and easy to parse at a glance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and acts as an invisible wrapper around the main content column on the right. Conceptually, it spans the full height of the page and the entire width of the right content area (approximately 78–82% of the viewport), sitting immediately to the right of the fixed left navigation column. It does not introduce any spacing, borders, or visible elements; instead, it simply ensures that data is available to the search form, article lists, and footer components that are visually rendered within the main content region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual data layer; occupies no visible space and does not affect layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the right-hand main content area, the featured articles section sits below the search form and category quick links. It begins roughly 64–80px beneath the search bar (respecting the spacious editorial feel) and is left-aligned with the search input’s text field edge. The section occupies about 70–80% of the main content width, leaving a generous margin on the far right to echo the blank, open canvas from the mockup. Vertically, its heading (if present) appears at the top of the block, followed by one to three featured article cards stacked downward with 24–32px between them. Each card has comfortable internal padding (16–24px) and is separated from the section boundaries by at least 16px. The bottom of this section leaves a 32–48px gap before the latest-articles-by-category section begins, maintaining the low-density rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the main content column; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is positioned directly beneath the featured-articles-section in the right main column, with a clear vertical gap of approximately 32–48px so the two sections feel distinct. It shares the same left alignment as the featured section and search form, and uses a similar width—about 70–80% of the main content area—leaving the right margin open. Within the section, category headings are stacked vertically, each followed by a compact list of recent articles in that category. Category blocks are separated by 24–32px of vertical space. The overall section height will typically extend through the lower half of the viewport and beyond, forming the main scrollable reading area. A generous 48–64px margin at the bottom separates this section from the footer newsletter form, accentuating the transition from article content to site-level engagement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content; remains within the main right column with no special positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category quick links appear in the right-hand column, just below the homepage search form. They form a compact horizontal row of links or pills aligned to the left edge of the search input, starting about 16–24px beneath the bottom of the search field. The row spans roughly 60–70% of the main content width, mirroring the width of the search form, and may wrap onto a second line if there are many categories, with 8–12px vertical spacing between lines. Individual links have modest padding (around 8px horizontal, 4–8px vertical) and 8–16px of horizontal spacing between them, maintaining the low-density feel without creating visual clutter. This band of links acts as a shallow navigation layer above the featured articles section, with about 32–40px of space before the featured content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the main content flow; scrolls with the page and anchors visually to the search form above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage search form is the first interactive element in the right content area and sits just below the thin horizontal rule that defines the top of the main canvas. The top of the search field is positioned approximately 24–32px beneath this rule, aligned to the left edge of the main content column (roughly where the right edge of the left sidebar begins). The search input itself spans about 60–70% of the main content width, leaving a wide, unoccupied strip on the right to echo the airy aesthetic. Vertically, the search field is tall enough to feel substantial (e.g., 40–48px high), with generous internal padding that aligns to the 8px spacing system. Optional labels or helper text, if present, sit immediately above or below the input with 8–12px spacing, but overall the block remains minimal. A clear 16–24px gap beneath the input makes room for the category-quick-links band, and a total of about 64–80px separates the search form from the featured-articles-section below, framing search as a distinct, prominent entry point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page (not sticky), keeping the layout calm and uncluttered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search suggestion list appears only when the user interacts with the homepage search form. Visually, it is an overlay panel directly anchored to the bottom edge of the search input in the right content column, sharing the same left alignment and width as the input field (about 60–70% of the main content width). It drops down into the space that would otherwise be reserved for category quick links and the top of the featured articles section, temporarily covering them while visible. The panel has subtle borders or a faint shadow to stand out from the white background while still feeling light. Internally, each suggestion line has 8–12px vertical padding and is separated by 4–8px of whitespace, with a comfortable 16–24px margin around the list’s inner edges. The list height is capped at roughly 40–50% of the viewport height, with internal scrolling if there are many suggestions, so it does not dominate the page. When hidden, it occupies no space and the underlying category quick links and content reclaim their full layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely or relatively positioned as an overlay anchored to the search form; it does not push other content down and disappears entirely when not in use.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global header navigation forms the left vertical sidebar that defines the page’s character. It occupies a fixed-width column along the entire left edge of the viewport, taking up roughly 18–22% of the screen width (for example, 260–300px on desktop). At the very top of this column, aligned with the page’s left and top edges, sits the ornate green plaque logo, centered horizontally within the sidebar. The logo block has generous breathing room: at least 24–32px of padding above and below, ensuring it stands out as the first visual anchor. Directly beneath the logo, separated by about 16–24px, is a vertical stack of all-caps navigation links. Each link is one line tall, left-aligned, and spaced evenly with approximately 8–12px of vertical padding and an additional 8–16px of vertical gap between items. One item such as 'OUR RIDES' includes a subtle down-caret icon to its right to signal expandable subnavigation; any revealed subitems are indented slightly to the right and spaced more tightly, but still maintain at least 4–8px of vertical separation. The navigation typically occupies the upper half of the sidebar’s height, with the lower half left intentionally blank. The right edge of this sidebar visually aligns with the starting point of the main content column and the thin horizontal rule that stretches across the top of the right-hand area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a fixed or sticky left column on desktop so that it remains visible as the user scrolls the main content. Its width stays constant while the right content area scrolls independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer newsletter form sits near the bottom of the right-hand main content column, below the latest-articles-by-category section. A generous vertical gap—approximately 48–64px—separates it from the articles above, marking a clear transition from reading to engagement. The form is centered within the main content area but still respects the content alignment: its main text and input fields are aligned with the same left margin as the articles and search form, while the overall block spans around 60–70% of the main content width. The heading or prompt appears at the top of this block with 8–16px of spacing before the input row. The email input field and subscribe button sit on a single line on larger screens, collectively occupying the majority of the block’s width, with 8–16px spacing between them. Internal padding of 16–24px and a bit of extra whitespace (24–32px) beneath the form separate it from the footer navigation links that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal vertical flow of the main content column; scrolls into view as the user reaches the bottom of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer navigation links appear directly below the footer newsletter form in the right content column, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. They are visually lighter and smaller than the primary navigation in the left sidebar, emphasizing their tertiary role. The links can be arranged in a single horizontal row centered within the main content width or as a short vertical list aligned with the same left margin as the newsletter text. In either case, each link has 4–8px of horizontal or vertical spacing to avoid crowding, and the entire footer nav block has 16–24px of top and bottom padding. The bottom of this block aligns with the page’s visual floor, with at least 32px of whitespace beneath it before the viewport edge, keeping the page from feeling cramped at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the end of the main content column; scrolls with the page and does not stick to the bottom of the viewport.

name: Article List
title: Articles
description: Lists blog articles about the horse for a selected category (training, health, competitions, or gear), with filtering, sorting, saving to a reading list, favoriting, pagination, and newsletter subscription.

core_features: Load and display articles for the category specified by the URL parameter `categoryId` using `getArticlesForCategory`.
Load available tag, year, and sort options for the current category using `getArticleFilterOptions` and render filter controls.
Allow users to refine the list by tag and year, and to change the sort order; changes are reflected in URL parameters (`tagId`, `year`, `sortOrder`, `page`).
Provide pagination for the article list based on `page` and API paging information, updating the `page` URL parameter on navigation.
Allow users to save or remove articles from their reading list directly in the list using `saveArticleToReadingList` and `removeArticleFromReadingList`.
Allow users to add or remove articles from favorites directly in the list using `addArticleToFavorites` and `removeArticleFromFavorites`.
Allow navigation into an article detail page by clicking the article title, passing `articleId` in the URL to `article_detail.html`.
Provide header navigation to other main pages (home, category tabs, photos, contact, reading list, favorites) using the defined outgoing connections.
Provide a header search form that navigates to `search_results.html` with `query`, optional `category`, and paging parameters.
Provide a footer newsletter subscription form that creates a newsletter subscription via `createNewsletterSubscription`.
user_workflows: Browse category articles: 1) User opens `articles.html` with `categoryId` in the URL. 2) Page controller reads URL parameters (`categoryId`, `tagId`, `year`, `sortOrder`, `page`). 3) Page calls `getArticleFilterOptions(categoryId)` to get filter choices. 4) Page calls `getArticlesForCategory(categoryId, filterTagId=tagId, filterYear=year, sortOrder, page, pageSize)` to retrieve the article list. 5) The article list component renders articles with titles, summaries, metadata, and states for reading list and favorites.

Filter by tag: 1) User selects a tag in the tag filter control. 2) Filter component updates the `tagId` parameter in the URL, sets `page` to 1, and triggers navigation to `articles.html` with the updated parameters. 3) On load, the page controller uses the new `tagId` to call `getArticlesForCategory` and `getArticleFilterOptions`. 4) The article list refreshes to show only articles matching the selected tag.

Filter by year (archive): 1) User selects a year in the year filter control. 2) Filter component updates the `year` parameter in the URL, sets `page` to 1, and navigates to the same page with updated parameters. 3) Page reloads data via `getArticlesForCategory` with `filterYear=year`. 4) Article list updates to show only articles from that year.

Change sort order: 1) User selects a sort option (e.g., newest first) in the sort control. 2) Sort component updates the `sortOrder` parameter in the URL and sets `page` to 1. 3) Page reloads and calls `getArticlesForCategory` with the new `sortOrder`. 4) Article list re-renders in the new order.

Use pagination: 1) User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or specific page number). 2) Pagination component updates the `page` parameter in the URL while keeping `categoryId`, `tagId`, `year`, and `sortOrder` unchanged. 3) Page reloads and calls `getArticlesForCategory` with the new `page`. 4) Article list shows the corresponding slice of results.

Save or remove an article from the reading list: 1) User clicks the reading-list toggle (e.g., save icon) on an article card. 2) If `is_in_reading_list` is false, the list component calls `saveArticleToReadingList(articleId)`, then updates the article’s saved state based on the response. 3) If `is_in_reading_list` is true, it calls `removeArticleFromReadingList(articleId)` and updates the state. 4) Optionally, the total reading list count indicator in the header is updated if it is displayed.

Favorite or unfavorite an article: 1) User clicks the favorite toggle on an article card. 2) If `is_favorited` is false, the list component calls `addArticleToFavorites(articleId)` and updates the favorite state after success. 3) If `is_favorited` is true, it calls `removeArticleFromFavorites(articleId)` and updates the favorite state. 4) Optional favorites counter in the header can be updated using the `total_favorites` value from responses.

Open an article detail: 1) User clicks on an article title or main clickable area. 2) Article list component reads the article’s `id` and navigates to `article_detail.html?articleId=<id>`. 3) The article detail page then handles its own data loading.

Use header search from the article list: 1) User types a search term into the header search field and optionally chooses a category filter there if available. 2) User submits the form. 3) Header search component builds a URL to `search_results.html` with `query`, optional `category`, `page=1`, and default `pageSize`. 4) Browser navigates to the search results page.

Subscribe to the newsletter: 1) User scrolls to the page footer and fills in the newsletter form fields (name, email, topic checkboxes, frequency, email format). 2) User submits the form. 3) Newsletter component calls `createNewsletterSubscription` with the entered values. 4) On success, component shows a confirmation state; on failure, it shows an error state and allows correction and resubmission.
interactions: On initial page load or whenever the URL changes: the page controller reads `categoryId`, `tagId`, `year`, `sortOrder`, and `page` from the URL, calls `getArticleFilterOptions` with `categoryId`, and calls `getArticlesForCategory` with the full filter/sort/paging state.

Clicking a category link in the header (Training, Health, Competitions, Gear) updates the URL to `articles.html?categoryId=<category>` (and optionally resets `tagId`, `year`, `sortOrder`, `page`) to switch categories.
Selecting a tag in the tag filter updates the `tagId` URL parameter and resets `page` to 1; it does not directly call interfaces but triggers a navigation that leads to new interface calls.
Selecting a year in the year filter updates the `year` URL parameter and resets `page` to 1, driving a new data load via `getArticlesForCategory` after navigation.
Changing the sort option updates the `sortOrder` URL parameter and resets `page` to 1; data is reloaded with the new sort order.
Clicking pagination controls updates only the `page` URL parameter while preserving `categoryId`, `tagId`, `year`, and `sortOrder` values; a new page of results is then loaded.
Clicking an article title or main clickable area navigates to `article_detail.html` with the article’s `id` as `articleId` in the URL.

Clicking the reading list toggle on an article card calls `saveArticleToReadingList` or `removeArticleFromReadingList` depending on the current `is_in_reading_list` state, then updates that state in the UI.
Clicking the favorite toggle on an article card calls `addArticleToFavorites` or `removeArticleFromFavorites` depending on the current `is_favorited` state, then updates that state in the UI.
Clicking the 'Reading list' header link navigates to `reading_list.html`.
Clicking the 'Favorites' header link navigates to `favorites.html`.
Clicking 'Home' in the header navigates to `index.html`.
Clicking 'Photos' in the header navigates to `gallery.html`.
Clicking 'Contact' in the header navigates to `contact.html`.
Submitting the header search form navigates to `search_results.html` with `query`, optional `category`, `page=1`, and default `pageSize` in the URL.
Clicking 'About' in the footer navigates to `about.html`.
Submitting the newsletter form in the footer calls `createNewsletterSubscription`, then shows success or error feedback based on the response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary source of state for this page is the URL query string. The required `categoryId` parameter selects which category of articles is shown and is passed to both `getArticleFilterOptions` and `getArticlesForCategory`. Optional parameters refine and control the list: `tagId` (mapped to `filterTagId` in `getArticlesForCategory`) restricts articles by tag, `year` (mapped to `filterYear`) restricts by publish year, `sortOrder` controls the ordering, and `page` controls pagination. On every load, the page controller parses these parameters, applies minimal validation (e.g., ensuring `page` is a positive integer), and then passes them into the interfaces. If optional parameters are absent, the page calls the interfaces without those filters so that the backend’s defaults apply. UI components never store persistent filter state locally; instead, any change to tag, year, sort, or page is reflected by updating the URL parameters and triggering navigation back to `articles.html` with the updated query string. This ensures that the page is always fully determined by the URL, supports bookmarking and sharing, and avoids using localStorage for state. Reading list and favorite states for articles come from `getArticlesForCategory` (`is_in_reading_list`, `is_favorited`) and are updated in response to `saveArticleToReadingList`, `removeArticleFromReadingList`, `addArticleToFavorites`, and `removeArticleFromFavorites` calls. The newsletter form does not affect list state and uses its own transient form state for one submission at a time.

components.functionality: Orchestrates the Article List page: reads URL parameters, calls `getArticleFilterOptions` and `getArticlesForCategory`, distributes data to child components, and listens for child events that require URL updates or interface calls.

Provides main navigation links to categories (training, health, competitions, gear) and other pages (home, photos, contact, reading list, favorites) and exposes the current category as active based on `categoryId`.
Accepts a search term and optional category filter from the user and navigates to the search results page with appropriate URL parameters.

Displays the current category name and an optional description based on data from `getArticleFilterOptions` or `getArticlesForCategory`, and can show a count of total articles from `getArticlesForCategory.total_results`.

Renders tag filter options, year filter options, and sort options sourced from `getArticleFilterOptions`. Reflects current selections based on URL parameters and notifies the page controller when the user changes filters or sort order so the URL can be updated.

Shows a concise summary of the currently applied tag, year, and sort order using `getArticlesForCategory.applied_filters`, and provides controls to clear individual filters or reset all filters by updating the URL.

Displays the list of articles returned from `getArticlesForCategory.articles`. Each item shows title, summary, publish date, tags, and reading time when available, along with controls for saving to the reading list and favoriting. It also indicates current saved/favorited state using `is_in_reading_list` and `is_favorited`.

Represents a single article within the list. Receives an article object, tag list, and boolean flags for `is_in_reading_list` and `is_favorited`. Emits events when the title is clicked or when the user toggles reading list or favorite status.

Renders pagination based on `getArticlesForCategory.page`, `getArticlesForCategory.page_size`, and `getArticlesForCategory.total_results`. When the user selects a new page, it signals the page controller to update the `page` URL parameter while preserving other filters.

Handles requests to save or remove articles from the reading list. When triggered by article list item events, it calls `saveArticleToReadingList` or `removeArticleFromReadingList` and returns updated state (including optional total count) back to the invoking component.

Handles requests to favorite or unfavorite articles. When triggered, it calls `addArticleToFavorites` or `removeArticleFromFavorites` and updates the corresponding article list item and any favorites count display.

Collects newsletter subscription details (name, email, selected topics, frequency, and email format) and submits them via `createNewsletterSubscription`. It manages submission state, success, and error feedback.
Provides footer navigation, including a link to the About page, and any other non-interfering footer links. Clicking About navigates to `about.html`.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a two-column layout mirroring the mockup: a narrow, fixed-width left rail for logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, mostly open right canvas for the article list. All interactive blog controls (category title, search, filters, articles, pagination, newsletter) are stacked vertically in the right column, while the left column stays structurally simple and static. The right column begins with a thin horizontal rule near the top, creating a subtle header band and establishing a visual starting line for the content below.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a two-column layout mirroring the mockup: a narrow, fixed-width left rail for logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, mostly open right canvas for the article list. All interactive blog controls (category title, search, filters, articles, pagination, newsletter) are stacked vertically in the right column, while the left column stays structurally simple and static. The right column begins with a thin horizontal rule near the top, creating a subtle header band and establishing a visual starting line for the content below.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The left navigation column occupies roughly 18–22% of the viewport width with a minimum practical width for legibility, while the right content area takes the remaining 78–82%. Within the right area, a slim top band (about 10–15% of the vertical space above the fold) is reserved for the horizontal rule and the first row containing the category header and search form. Below that, the majority of vertical space is dedicated to the article list, with filters and applied filters occupying a small strip of space above it, and pagination plus newsletter/footer content sitting comfortably in the lower portion of the canvas. Significant whitespace is left on the right side of the article column, so the list itself occupies only about 60–70% of the right column width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays very low. Major stacks (logo, nav, filter strip, article list, pagination, newsletter) are separated by 32–48px vertical gaps. Individual controls such as filters, pagination buttons, and actions use padding in the 8–16px range. Article list items breathe with 24px internal padding and 32–40px separation between items, keeping the reading experience calm and uncluttered. There are no visually heavy cards or dense panels; instead, simple text blocks and light rules provide structure.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Attention starts at the ornate green logo at the top-left, then moves down through the vertical navigation in the left rail. The eye is then guided to the right by the horizontal rule near the top of the content area, where it first meets the category header and nearby search box. From there, the flow continues down the right column: filters, applied filters summary, then the vertically stacked article titles, each acting as an anchor for deeper reading. Secondary interactions like reading-list and favorite actions are available but visually subdued. Finally, the eye lands on pagination controls and the low-key newsletter and footer links at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: The page appears as a quiet, editorial-style canvas with most functional elements anchored in the upper-left quadrant. A narrow left sidebar holds the decorative green plaque logo at the very top, followed by a tall column of simple, all-caps navigation links, one of which shows a small downward caret hinting at subpages. The rest of this column remains largely empty, reinforcing the minimalist aesthetic. To the right, a wide, airy content area stretches across the screen. Near its top, a thin horizontal rule runs from left to right, visually aligning with the logo’s lower edge and lightly separating a subtle header band from the main reading area. Below this line, a row places the current category title toward the left and a compact search field to the right, both with generous breathing room. Underneath, a delicate filters strip and a low-key applied-filters summary sit above a single, left-aligned column of article entries that occupies only part of the right area, leaving comfortable whitespace to its right. Each article reads like a small editorial block: title, metadata, a short excerpt, and unobtrusive icons to save or favorite. At the bottom of the article stack, simple pagination controls spread out horizontally, followed by a slim newsletter subscription form and a sparse footer link row. Throughout, the layout maintains a calm vertical rhythm, guiding readers downward through one primary content column, while the left navigation rail and top rule quietly frame the experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller establishes a two-column, full-viewport layout using a horizontal flex or similar structure. The left column is a fixed-width vertical rail anchored to the left edge of the viewport, occupying approximately 18–22% of the window width (with a sensible minimum width around 240–280px). The right column fills the remaining 78–82% and extends from the top of the viewport to the bottom, serving as a mostly open canvas. Vertical scrolling affects the right column’s content, while the left rail appears visually stable, contributing to a quiet, anchored frame for the blog. All internal spacing respects an 8px-based scale, with major vertical separations between stacked sections in the right column using 32–48px margins to preserve an airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a two-column layout with a visually fixed-feeling left rail and a scrollable right content area; the page height stretches to at least 100% of the viewport, with background remaining clean and white.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation component occupies the entire left column from the top, forming a tall vertical rail. At the very top-left of the page, it reserves a block roughly 96–128px tall for the decorative green plaque logo, centered horizontally within the rail with generous top and bottom padding (16–24px) to let it breathe. Directly beneath the logo, a vertical stack of navigation links begins. Each nav item is rendered in one line of all-caps text, left-aligned, with about 8–12px vertical padding and 0–16px horizontal padding from the left edge, all aligned to the 8px grid. Items are evenly spaced down the upper half of the sidebar, leaving a large empty band occupying the lower 40–50% of the column. One of the items (e.g., 'OUR RIDES') displays a small downward caret icon just to the right of its label, aligned with the text baseline, hinting at expandable subnavigation. The entire navigation area remains clean, with no heavy backgrounds, relying on spacing and typography for structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Visually behaves as a fixed left sidebar: it stays aligned to the left edge and top of the viewport as the user scrolls the right-hand content; the nav itself does not scroll independently unless the viewport becomes very short.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form sits in the right content column near the top, sharing a horizontal row with the category header. This row is placed approximately 32px below the thin horizontal rule that marks the top of the content area. Within this row, the search form is aligned to the right edge of the content column, occupying about 30–40% of the column’s width, with a simple, single-line text field and possibly a subtle icon. It has 8–12px internal padding and minimal border styling to keep it light. There is at least 24px of whitespace below this row before the filters appear, reinforcing the low-density, editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stays inline within the top content row of the right column; moves naturally with page scroll and aligns to the right of the category header within the same vertical alignment line.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-header appears at the top of the right column’s main content, just below the thin horizontal rule. It is left-aligned and sits in the same horizontal row as the header-search-form. This row is positioned about 32px beneath the rule, forming a clear starting point for the article list’s context. The category title (e.g., 'TRAINING', 'HEALTH') is rendered in a slightly larger or bolder font than body text, while still maintaining the understated aesthetic. It occupies roughly 50–60% of the content column’s width on the left, leaving space for the search form on the right. There is 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of this row and the article-filters-bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the top content row; scrolls with the main content and anchors the semantic starting point of the article list area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-filters-bar is positioned directly below the category-header/search row, separated by about 24–32px of whitespace. It is a low-profile horizontal strip of filter controls aligned to the left edge of the main article column. The bar spans roughly 70–80% of the content column width but remains visually light, with filter options arranged in a single row (e.g., category chips, sort dropdown, date range) spaced 16–24px apart. Controls have compact but comfortable padding (around 8–12px vertically and 12–16px horizontally). There is no heavy background; at most, a subtle, thin rule or muted text labels delineate the area. A gap of about 16px below the filters bar separates it from the applied-filters-summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, left-aligned strip within the right column; scrolls with the page content and maintains consistent horizontal alignment with the article list below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-summary sits immediately below the article-filters-bar, with roughly 16px of vertical spacing between them. It appears as a single line or short block of subdued text summarizing active filters (e.g., 'Sorted by newest • Tag: Competitions'). It is left-aligned to the same vertical axis as the article titles, occupying about 60–70% of the content column width and leaving whitespace to the right. The text is smaller or lighter than body text to keep it secondary. A 24–32px gap separates this summary from the first article in the article-list, marking a gentle transition between control elements and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block within the main content flow; scrolls with the article list and does not change layout dynamically beyond its text length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list occupies the central vertical band of the right column and is the main reading area. It begins around 24–32px below the applied-filters-summary. The list is a single column of articles, left-aligned, with the column width constrained to a comfortable reading measure (approximately 640–760px) even when the right content area is wider. This leaves a noticeable strip of empty space on the right side of the content area, reinforcing the minimalist, editorial feeling. Articles are stacked vertically with 32–40px of whitespace between each article-list-item. The list itself extends downward, potentially beyond the viewport height, with the left navigation rail providing a stable visual anchor while the article column scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard scrollable vertical list within the right content column; maintains a fixed left alignment and a max-width constraint so that it never stretches edge-to-edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article-list-item is a light, text-first block within the article-list column. It spans the full width of the article list’s constrained column (but not the entire right content area) and uses internal padding of about 24px on all sides to give breathing room to the title, metadata, excerpt, and action controls. The article title is at the top, left-aligned, with generous line spacing; below it sits a small line of meta information (date, category, reading time), followed by a short excerpt. At either the top-right corner (aligned with the title) or in a small horizontal row at the bottom-right of the item, the reading-list-actions and favorite-actions appear as small icon-and-label controls, kept visually subtle with light strokes or simple outlines. Separation between items is created by 32–40px of vertical margin and optionally a very thin bottom rule or soft shadow, but large card borders are avoided to stay true to the minimalist editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Laid out as block-level elements within the article list column; they expand vertically with content but maintain consistent padding and alignment so the column reads as a smooth editorial flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-pagination component is positioned below the final article-list-item, separated from it by around 32–40px of vertical space. It is left-aligned with the article list column and uses a horizontal arrangement of pagination controls (e.g., 'Previous', page numbers, 'Next'). Controls are spaced 16–24px apart and use 8–12px vertical padding to keep them tappable but visually understated. The entire pagination row occupies only the width needed for its controls, leaving ample whitespace to the right. Below the pagination, a larger gap of 40–48px separates it from the footer-newsletter-form, subtly signaling the end of the current article set.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right column’s vertical flow; scrolls naturally and remains left-aligned with the article column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-actions component is embedded inside each article-list-item as part of a small actions cluster. It is typically placed at the top-right corner of the item, aligned with the article title, or in a compact row at the bottom-right, depending on the chosen micro-layout. The control may appear as a simple icon (e.g., bookmark) accompanied by a short label such as 'Save'. It is small but given at least 8–12px of padding around the icon to maintain touchability. Visually, it is understated: thin lines, no heavy fill, maintaining the quiet feel of the card. It sits on the same horizontal line as the favorite-actions if both are present, with about 16px of space between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each article card’s internal layout; stays fixed relative to the article content and moves as part of the article-list-item when the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-actions component is paired with the reading-list-actions within each article-list-item. It appears as a small icon (e.g., heart) with an optional short label such as 'Favorite', placed on the same horizontal axis as the reading-list action, typically to its right with a 16px gap. It shares the same visual style—light, minimal lines and moderate padding—so the actions cluster reads as a unified micro-component. Positioned at either the top-right or bottom-right of the article card, this cluster is visually secondary to the article title and text, ensuring the reading experience remains primary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within each article card; its position is anchored within the card’s action row and it scrolls with the surrounding article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-newsletter-form is located near the bottom of the right content column, starting roughly 40–48px below the article-pagination. It is left-aligned with the article list column but can extend slightly wider, up to 70–80% of the content column width, to accommodate an email input and a subscribe button on a single line on larger screens. The form sits within a light, unobtrusive container that may use a thin outline or subtle background tint, with 24px of internal padding to maintain the spacious aesthetic. The heading or call-to-action text appears on the left, with the email field and button either on the same line to the right or stacked with 16px vertical spacing on narrower screens. There is ample whitespace (32–40px) below this form before the footer-links, ensuring it feels like a gentle closing invitation rather than a crowded footer block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline section near the bottom of the right column; scrolls with content and maintains alignment with the main article column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-links component sits at the very bottom of the right content column, approximately 32–40px below the footer-newsletter-form. It is a simple row or two of small, understated text links (e.g., 'About', 'Contact', 'Privacy'), left-aligned with the article column. The links are spaced 16–24px apart horizontally, with minimal vertical stacking if needed. There is no heavy footer bar; instead, the links appear to rest lightly at the bottom of the canvas, with additional whitespace below if the viewport is tall. This keeps the page feeling open and editorial rather than boxed in.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard bottom-of-content section; appears after all other main content in the scroll flow and does not fix itself to the viewport bottom, allowing the overall page to feel natural and content-first.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays the full content of a single article and lets users save it, favorite it, comment, participate in a contextual poll, share the link, navigate via breadcrumbs, and subscribe to the newsletter.

core_features: Load and display a single article’s full details (title, content, images, category, tags, published date, breadcrumbs) using getArticleDetail(articleId).
Show current reading-list state and allow adding/removing the article from the reading list using saveArticleToReadingList and removeArticleFromReadingList.
Show current favorite state and allow adding/removing the article from favorites using addArticleToFavorites and removeArticleFromFavorites.
Provide a share area that allows users to copy the article URL to their clipboard.
Display existing comments for the article and provide a form to submit new comments, conditionally available when article.allow_comments is true, using submitArticleComment.
Display a contextual poll in the sidebar (if provided by getArticleDetail), allowing users to vote on one option using submitPollVote and then view updated results.
Allow users to navigate via breadcrumbs to the homepage, category/tag listings, and archive views using data returned by getArticleDetail.
Provide a footer newsletter subscription form that creates a subscription using createNewsletterSubscription.
user_workflows:
View article details:
1. User opens article_detail.html with a URL parameter articleId.
2. Page reads articleId from the URL.
3. Page calls getArticleDetail(articleId).
4. When data returns, page displays article title, content, hero image (if present), category, tags, published date, and breadcrumb trail.
5. Page also initializes reading-list and favorite toggle states from is_in_reading_list and is_favorited, displays comments, and shows a poll if included in the response.

Toggle article in reading list:
1. User clicks the reading-list toggle control in the article meta area.
2. If the article is not currently in the reading list (is_in_reading_list is false), the page calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId).
3. On success, the component updates the local state to is_in_reading_list = true and may display the updated count returned in total_items.
4. If the article is already in the reading list (is_in_reading_list is true), the page calls removeArticleFromReadingList(articleId).
5. On success, the component updates the local state to is_in_reading_list = false and may display the updated total_items.
6. No page reload is required; the button label/icon updates based on the new state.

Toggle article as favorite:
1. User clicks the favorite toggle control near the article title.
2. If the article is not currently favorited (is_favorited is false), the page calls addArticleToFavorites(articleId).
3. On success, the component updates the local state to is_favorited = true and may use total_favorites for internal counters.
4. If the article is already favorited (is_favorited is true), the page calls removeArticleFromFavorites(articleId).
5. On success, the component updates the local state to is_favorited = false.
6. Button label/icon changes immediately to reflect the new status.

Share article link:
1. User clicks a “Copy link” control in the share area.
2. The component reads article.url from the loaded article object.
3. The component uses browser clipboard functionality to copy article.url.
4. A non-blocking confirmation state is shown (e.g., a transient message) without using any additional interfaces.

Submit a new comment on an article:
1. Page loads with article.allow_comments evaluated from the getArticleDetail response.
2. If allow_comments is true, the comment form is displayed; otherwise, a message indicates comments are closed and the form is not shown.
3. User fills in Name, Email, and Comment fields.
4. User submits the form.
5. The page calls submitArticleComment with parameters: articleId, author_name (from Name field), author_email (from Email field), and body (from Comment field).
6. On success, the component updates its comments list using the comments array returned by the interface and clears the form inputs.
7. If the backend uses moderation (is_approved field), the UI relies solely on the returned comments list to determine which comments are visible, without making assumptions about approval timing.

Participate in the sidebar poll:
1. Page loads and getArticleDetail returns optional poll data, including poll, options, is_active, user_has_voted, user_selected_option_id, and total_votes.
2. If no poll data is present, the poll component is not rendered.
3. If poll data is present and poll.is_active is true:
   - If user_has_voted is false, the poll widget displays options as selectable inputs.
   - If user_has_voted is true, the component displays results (e.g., vote counts or percentages derived from options.vote_count and total_votes).
4. When a user selects an option and submits their vote, the page calls submitPollVote(pollId, optionId).
5. On success, the poll widget updates its internal poll state (poll, options, total_votes, user_selected_option_id) based on the response.
6. After voting, user_has_voted is treated as true in the UI and the results view is displayed.

Navigate via breadcrumbs and article metadata:
1. Page displays breadcrumbs derived from the breadcrumbs array in the getArticleDetail response.
2. When the user clicks a breadcrumb of type "home", the browser navigates to index.html with no extra parameters.
3. When the user clicks a breadcrumb of type "category", the browser navigates to articles.html with a categoryId parameter derived from the current article’s category and optionally a year parameter from article.published_at if the breadcrumb represents an archive.
4. When the user clicks a breadcrumb of type "tag", the browser navigates to articles.html with categoryId (from article.category) and tagId (from the selected Tag.id). A year parameter may be added if the breadcrumb corresponds to an archive view.
5. When the user clicks a breadcrumb of type "article", the browser remains on the current page (no navigation) because the detail page is already displayed.

Use header search from article detail:
1. User enters a search term in the header search input and optionally selects a category.
2. User submits the search form.
3. The page navigates to search_results.html with URL parameters: query (from input), category (from optional select), page = 1, and pageSize = default (set by implementation but not hardcoded in content).
4. Article detail page does not directly call any search interface; it only constructs navigation based on user input.

Open related pages from header/footer and article meta:
1. User can navigate to reading_list.html by clicking the reading list link in the header; no parameters are required.
2. User can navigate to favorites.html by clicking the favorites link in the header; no parameters are required.
3. User can navigate to gallery.html by clicking the photos link in the header; no parameters are required.
4. User can navigate to about.html by clicking an About link in the footer; no parameters are required.
5. If a “Contact about this article” link is present in the article meta area, clicking it navigates to contact.html with relatedArticleId set to the current articleId.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the footer:
1. User scrolls to the footer and sees a newsletter subscription form.
2. User enters their name and email and selects topic preferences (mapped to wants_training_tips, wants_competition_updates, wants_merchandise_offers), frequency, and email_format.
3. User submits the form.
4. The page calls createNewsletterSubscription with name, email, the chosen topic flags, frequency, and email_format.
5. On success, the component may show a confirmation state using the message field and optionally disable the form or clear inputs.
6. No other interfaces are invoked from this form on this page.
interactions: Initial data load: On page load (or when articleId in the URL changes), call getArticleDetail(articleId) and bind the returned article, tags, breadcrumbs, comments, reading-list state, favorite state, and poll data to the respective components.

Reading-list toggle click: Clicking the reading-list toggle button decides between saveArticleToReadingList(articleId) and removeArticleFromReadingList(articleId) based on the current is_in_reading_list state and updates the displayed state after a successful response.

Favorite toggle click: Clicking the favorite toggle button decides between addArticleToFavorites(articleId) and removeArticleFromFavorites(articleId) based on the current is_favorited state and updates the displayed state after a successful response.

Share control click: Clicking the copy-link control reads article.url from the bound article object and uses browser APIs to copy it to the clipboard, with a transient success/error state handled entirely on the client side.
Comment form input: User types into Name, Email, and Comment inputs; the component maintains local form state until submission.

Comment form submit: On submit, the component validates required fields, then calls submitArticleComment(articleId, author_name, author_email, body); on success it replaces its comments list with the returned comments array and clears the form.
Poll option selection: User selects a poll option (e.g., via radio button); the selection updates local pollOptionSelected state but does not call any interface yet.

Poll vote submission: User submits their selected option; the component calls submitPollVote(pollId, optionId). On success it updates the poll and options state with the response and switches the view to a results-focused layout that uses updated vote_count and total_votes.

Breadcrumb click: Clicking a breadcrumb element triggers client-side navigation. The target URL and parameters are computed from the breadcrumb’s type and the article/tag data already loaded from getArticleDetail.
Header navigation clicks: Clicking header links (Home, Photos, Reading list, Favorites) triggers navigation to the corresponding pages with the appropriate parameters (if any).
Header search submit: Submitting the search form triggers navigation to search_results.html with query, optional category, page, and pageSize parameters constructed from the form inputs.
Article meta link click: Clicking “Contact about this article” navigates to contact.html with relatedArticleId set to the current articleId.
Newsletter form input: User interacts with text inputs, checkboxes, and dropdowns for the newsletter; state is held in the newsletter form component.

Newsletter form submit: On submit, the component calls createNewsletterSubscription with the current form values. On success/failure it uses the returned success and message fields to update its feedback state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of page state is the articleId URL parameter. On each page load, the application reads articleId from the query string (for example, article_detail.html?articleId=...). If articleId is present, getArticleDetail(articleId) is called. All UI state that depends on the article (title, content, tags, category, breadcrumbs, comments, hero image, allow_comments, reading-list state, favorites state, and poll configuration) is derived from this response and kept in memory within the page components. If articleId is missing or invalid, the page should enter an error state and avoid calling the interfaces. Navigation to other pages is always performed by changing the browser URL and passing parameters through the query string; the article detail page does not store persistent state using localStorage or other client-side storage. Reading-list and favorites status are treated as per-request state returned by getArticleDetail (is_in_reading_list and is_favorited) and updated in memory after calling saveArticleToReadingList, removeArticleFromReadingList, addArticleToFavorites, and removeArticleFromFavorites. Comment list state is initially taken from getArticleDetail.comments and then replaced with the comments array returned from submitArticleComment after a successful submission. Poll state is initially taken from getArticleDetail.poll, including whether a user has already voted; after a vote, it is replaced with the poll and options data returned by submitPollVote. Newsletter subscription state is purely local to the footer form and is not persisted on the page beyond handling the createNewsletterSubscription response.

components.functionality: Provides global navigation from the article detail page to other main sections and includes a search form that redirects to search results.
event_handlers: onSubmitSearchForm (navigates to search_results.html with query, optional category, page=1, pageSize=default)

components.functionality: Reads articleId from the URL, calls getArticleDetail(articleId), and distributes the resulting data to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad (reads articleId from URL; if present, calls getArticleDetail(articleId); if missing/invalid, sets an error state)
onArticleIdChange (if the URL changes while on the page, recalls getArticleDetail with the new articleId)

components.functionality: Displays a breadcrumb trail for the current article and allows navigation to home, category, tag, and archive listing pages.
event_handlers: onClickBreadcrumbItem (if type='home', navigate to index.html; if type='category', navigate to articles.html with categoryId derived from article.category and optional year from article.published_at; if type='tag', navigate to articles.html with categoryId and tagId; if type='article', stay on current page)

components.functionality: Shows the article title, category label, primary tag (if any), and metadata such as published date and reading time using the data loaded from getArticleDetail.
event_handlers: onClickCategoryLabel (navigates to articles.html with categoryId based on article.category and optional year from article.published_at)
onClickPrimaryTagLabel (navigates to articles.html with categoryId from article.category and tagId from selected Tag.id)

components.functionality: Displays the article hero image if article.hero_image_url is provided.
Renders the full article content using article.content.
Displays article metadata (published date, category, tags) and hosts action controls including contact link, reading-list toggle, favorite toggle, and share actions.
event_handlers: onClickContactAboutArticle (navigates to contact.html with relatedArticleId set to current article.id)

onClickReadingListToggle (if is_in_reading_list is false, calls saveArticleToReadingList(article.id); on success, updates is_in_reading_list to true; if true, calls removeArticleFromReadingList(article.id); on success, updates is_in_reading_list to false)

onClickFavoriteToggle (if is_favorited is false, calls addArticleToFavorites(article.id); on success, updates is_favorited to true; if true, calls removeArticleFromFavorites(article.id); on success, updates is_favorited to false)
onClickShareCopyLink (reads article.url and copies it to clipboard; updates local feedback state)

components.functionality: Lists all tags associated with the article and allows navigation to the corresponding tag-filtered article listing.
event_handlers: onClickTag (navigates to articles.html with categoryId derived from article.category and tagId from clicked Tag.id)

components.functionality: Displays existing comments for the article and shows a comment form when comments are allowed.
event_handlers: onRender (checks article.allow_comments to decide whether to display the comment form)

onSubmitCommentForm (collects Name, Email, Comment fields and calls submitArticleComment(article.id, author_name, author_email, body); on success, replaces the displayed comments list with the returned comments array and clears the form)
onCommentsUpdated (re-renders comment list when comments array changes)

components.functionality: Displays a contextual poll when provided by getArticleDetail, allows users to vote on options when the poll is active, and shows updated results after voting.
event_handlers: onPollDataLoad (initializes internal state based on poll, options, is_active, user_has_voted, user_selected_option_id, total_votes from getArticleDetail)
onSelectPollOption (updates local state to track which optionId is currently selected before submission)

onSubmitPollVote (if poll.is_active is true and an option is selected, calls submitPollVote(poll.poll.id or equivalent pollId field, selected optionId; on success, updates poll and options state, total_votes, and user_selected_option_id; switches UI into results mode)
onPollClosed (if poll.is_active is false, ensures only the results view is shown and voting controls are disabled)

components.functionality: Provides share-related actions for the current article, currently focused on copying the article link.
event_handlers: onClickCopyLink (copies article.url to the clipboard and updates local feedback state)

components.functionality: Provides footer-level navigation to informational pages and contains the newsletter subscription form.
Collects user information and preferences to subscribe them to the newsletter.
event_handlers: onSubmitNewsletterForm (calls createNewsletterSubscription with name, email, wants_training_tips, wants_competition_updates, wants_merchandise_offers, frequency, email_format; on success, uses the message and success fields to show confirmation and optionally clear or disable the form)

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s left-rail layout: a fixed-width vertical header-navigation column on the far left and one large, mostly open content canvas on the right. All page-level interaction and reading (breadcrumbs, article, comments, poll, share, newsletter) lives within a single vertical flow in the right content area. Within that right area, the article itself becomes a narrower reading column centered/left-aligned inside the canvas, with occasional lightweight side-asides (like the poll) that do not break the primary vertical reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s left-rail layout: a fixed-width vertical header-navigation column on the far left and one large, mostly open content canvas on the right. All page-level interaction and reading (breadcrumbs, article, comments, poll, share, newsletter) lives within a single vertical flow in the right content area. Within that right area, the article itself becomes a narrower reading column centered/left-aligned inside the canvas, with occasional lightweight side-asides (like the poll) that do not break the primary vertical reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate ~20% of the viewport width to the left navigation column and ~80% to the right content area. Within the right content area, constrain the article reading column to roughly 60–70% of the available width (e.g., max 720–760px) and center it with generous horizontal margins, preserving the sense of an open page. Vertical spacing follows an 8px scale, with major section separations (between header, media, body, comments, etc.) using ~32–48px, and internal component padding around 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain very low density: plenty of white space around each block, no tight stacks of controls, and minimal decorative elements. Each component sits in its own vertical segment with clear breathing room, especially around text-heavy areas. Multi-element units (like meta actions or share controls) are grouped into short, single lines or compact clusters with 8–16px gaps, ensuring the page still feels calm and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the logo and navigation in the left column, then is guided horizontally by the top horizontal rule into the article area. Within the article column, the flow is purely top-to-bottom: breadcrumbs → title/meta → hero media → body → tags → comments → footer navigation → newsletter. Secondary elements like poll and share controls are placed to the side or just below key sections, lightly touched by the eye as it moves down, but never overpowering the main reading path.

layout.overall_description: The page is divided into two clear vertical zones: a narrow, fixed left sidebar and a wide, airy content canvas on the right. In the left column, a decorative green plaque logo sits at the very top, followed by a tall, evenly spaced stack of all-caps navigation links, echoing the mockup’s minimalist blog feel. A fine, light horizontal rule starts just to the right of the logo and runs across the top of the right content area, forming a subtle header band that visually separates navigation from the article space. The article itself lives inside a centered reading column within the right area, set in from the edges so there is generous white space on both sides. At the top of this column, a light breadcrumb trail leads into a large, quiet article title and lean meta strip. A full-width hero image for the article sits just below, then the main text flows downward in a comfortable line length. Off to the side of the body (within the main canvas, not in the nav area), a compact poll widget appears as an editorial sidebar block aligned with the mid-upper portion of the article. Farther down, subtle tag chips, comments, a slim share/actions region, next/previous navigation, and a simple newsletter form stack vertically with ample gaps, preserving the spacious, contemplative mood of the original mockup while supporting all required interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation component forms the entire left vertical sidebar. It occupies approximately 18–22% of the viewport width and stretches from the top edge to the bottom edge of the screen. At the very top-left, the ornate green plaque logo is anchored with around 24–32px padding from the top and left browser edges. Directly beneath the logo, with about 24px vertical gap, is a vertical stack of navigation links (one per line, all caps) that fills the upper half of the sidebar height. Each nav item has about 8–12px vertical padding and 0–16px horizontal padding, aligned to an 8px grid, and they are left-aligned under the logo. The 'OUR RIDES' item includes a subtle down-caret placed immediately to its right, on the same baseline, hinting at expandable sub-navigation but without opening an actual panel in this view. Below the nav, the lower half of the sidebar is deliberately left mostly empty, preserving the airy, editorial feel and emphasizing the concentration of interaction in the upper-left quadrant. On the right edge of this component, aligned roughly with the logo’s vertical center, begins the thin horizontal rule that runs across the top of the main content area, visually tying the sidebar to the right canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the left side of the viewport so it remains visible while the right-side content scrolls; internal content scrolls only if viewport height is very small.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-loader appears exclusively within the right content area. When active, it overlays or replaces the article column, occupying the same width as the intended article content (about 60–70% of the right content area’s width) and centered horizontally within the right canvas. Vertically, it is positioned starting just below the top horizontal rule, with 32–40px of space separating it from the rule, and extends downward only as needed for a skeleton or loading indicator. The surrounding margins (to the right content area edges and between loader elements) remain generous, reinforcing the low-density, minimal feel during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-sticky; appears inline in the main content flow, then is replaced by the actual article components once data is loaded.
