component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-trail is positioned at the very top of the article content column on the right, immediately below the horizontal rule that spans from the left sidebar. It starts roughly 32px beneath that rule. The breadcrumbs are left-aligned to the article text column, which sits offset from the right content area’s left edge by a generous margin (e.g., 10–15% of the total page width), ensuring an airy margin between the sidebar and the start of text. The breadcrumb line itself is subtle and compact, occupying a single line of text with 4–8px of vertical padding, and spanning no more than the width of the article column (not the full browser width). There is about 16–24px of vertical space below the breadcrumb line before the article header block begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline at the top of the main content flow; does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header-block sits directly beneath the breadcrumb-trail, aligned to the same left and right bounds as the article text column. It occupies the full width of that column, typically around 60–70% of the right content area, centered within the right canvas. The main title is large but not bombastic, with ample white space above (16–24px from the breadcrumb) and below (16–24px before the meta/actions row). Any subtitle or author/date information appears as a compact stack under the title with 8–12px spacing between lines. The entire block feels like a self-contained editorial masthead, with at least 32px of space above the hero media that follows, keeping the hierarchy clean and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow; scrolls naturally with the article.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-hero-media sits immediately below the article-header-block, separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the article content column, aligning its left and right edges with the header and body text. The media (likely an image of the horse) maintains a generous aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9 or taller) but does not bleed to the very edges of the browser; significant white margins remain on both sides to preserve the airy editorial layout. Below the media, there is a 16–24px gap before any caption, and 32–40px before the start of the article-content-body, maintaining a calm transition from image to text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline media block within the content column; scales responsively within the column but does not exceed its width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content-body forms the main reading column and is positioned directly beneath the hero media, with approximately 32–40px of vertical spacing between them. It shares the same left and right boundaries as the header and hero, forming a single, consistent column. The text width is intentionally limited to a comfortable reading measure (around 60–80 characters per line), leaving clear white margins on both sides within the right content area. Paragraphs are spaced with about 16–24px between them, and any subheadings or inline quotes are separated by slightly larger gaps (24–32px) to preserve clarity. The body runs vertically down the page, and as the user scrolls, this column remains the primary focus, with occasional side-asides (like the poll widget) aligned to its right but not encroaching into the text area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable text column; no sticky behavior. Other components like the poll and share controls are positioned relative to this column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-meta-actions cluster appears in two possible locations depending on exact composition; in this layout it is placed just below the article-header-block and above the hero media, acting as a lightweight control strip. It aligns to the same left edge as the title and spans only as wide as needed for its controls (save, favorite, comment count, etc.), typically less than the full column width. The controls are laid out horizontally in a single row, with 8–16px spacing between icons/labels, and 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the row, making the strip feel like a thin, understated band. There is around 16px of vertical space separating this meta row from the title above, and 24–32px before the hero image below, so that controls are clearly visible but do not crowd the main heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline band within the main column; scrolls with the article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tag-list-section is positioned at the end of the article-content-body, separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical space from the last paragraph. It occupies the same content column width and is left-aligned with the body text. Tags are displayed as a low-key horizontal wrap of pill-shaped or simple text chips, each with about 8–12px horizontal padding and 4–8px vertical padding, and 8px gaps between tags. The entire tag block has about 16px of internal padding and sits like a subtle footer for the article, with an additional 32–40px of whitespace below before the comments-section starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline; behaves as a concluding block for the article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comments-section appears directly below the tag-list-section, with roughly 32–48px of space between them, marking a clear transition from article to discussion. It still aligns with the article column’s left edge but may use a slightly greater effective width by allowing comment text to approach the column’s full width for readability. A simple heading such as 'Comments' appears at the top of the section, followed by a vertical stack of comment items separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing. The section maintains generous top and bottom padding (24–32px), and there is a substantial whitespace buffer of about 48px below the last comment (or reply form) before the article-footer-navigation begins, keeping the lower part of the page feeling open and unhurried.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked; scrolls as part of the main content flow with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The poll-sidebar-widget is treated as an editorial sidebar box located within the right content area but outside the strict article text column. On larger screens, it is positioned to the right of the article-content-body, roughly aligned vertically with the upper-middle portion of the article (e.g., starting around the second or third screenful of text, or approximately 1/3 down the article). It occupies about 20–25% of the right content area’s width, leaving a 16–24px horizontal gutter between it and the article text. The widget appears as a light, unobtrusive card with 16–24px internal padding and ample whitespace around question and options. On narrow screens, it collapses into the main vertical flow, appearing inline between paragraphs or just below the hero media, still separated by 32px of vertical space from adjacent content to maintain the low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, behaves as a floating aside aligned to the right of the article column and may be sticky within that viewport segment (e.g., staying visible for part of the scroll within the article body). On smaller screens, it becomes a regular inline block within the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The share-actions-panel is placed in the lower portion of the article area, just above the article-footer-navigation and after the comments-section, separated from comments by about 32–40px of space. It aligns to the same left edge as the article column and spans only as wide as needed for the share icons/labels, usually not exceeding half the column width. The share options are arranged horizontally in a single row, with 8–16px spacing between each icon, and 8–12px vertical padding for the entire row. The overall treatment is subtle and typographically light so it does not compete with the main narrative. A small heading like 'Share this ride' can appear above the icons with 8–12px spacing, still maintaining plenty of white space around the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline row; no sticky behavior. On very narrow screens it can wrap to two lines while preserving comfortable spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-footer-navigation sits near the bottom of the right content area, directly below the share-actions-panel with roughly 32–40px of vertical separation. It uses the full width of the article column, aligning to the same left and right edges as the body text. Within this block, links to the previous and next articles are arranged horizontally (previous on the left, next on the right) or stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing, depending on screen width. Each link area has 16–24px of internal padding and sits inside the same airy canvas with no heavy containers, maintaining the minimalist editorial feel. There is a significant whitespace buffer (32–48px) below this navigation before the newsletter-subscription-form, distinguishing navigation from subscription.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer band within the main content flow; not sticky and scrolls off-screen as the user continues down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-subscription-form is the final component in the right content area, positioned at the very bottom of the page content. It appears below the article-footer-navigation with approximately 32–48px of vertical space between them. The form is centered within the article column and may visually feel slightly wider, but still respects the same left and right bounds as the text. The layout is extremely simple: a brief, single-line invitation to subscribe centered or left-aligned at the top of the block, followed by a single email input and a primary button arranged horizontally on larger screens and stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing on smaller screens. The form container itself has 24–32px of internal padding and ample whitespace above and below, giving it a calm, low-pressure presence that fits the personal blog tone. The overall block ends with generous bottom margin (32–48px) before the page’s actual bottom, so the content doesn’t feel cramped against the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, bottom-of-page block within the main content flow; remains non-sticky and scrolls off-screen when the user scrolls up.

name: Reading List
title: Reading List
description: Shows all saved articles in the user’s reading list, with options to open, remove, sort, and group them, plus newsletter subscription from the footer.

core_features: Load the current reading list using getReadingListItems with groupBy and sortBy values derived from URL parameters.
Display saved articles either as a single flat list or grouped (e.g., by category) based on the response from getReadingListItems.
Allow users to open any saved article by clicking its title, navigating to article_detail.html with the corresponding articleId.
Allow users to remove individual articles from the reading list by calling removeArticleFromReadingList and updating the list view accordingly.
Provide controls to change grouping and sorting, updating URL parameters (groupBy, sortBy) and reloading the list via getReadingListItems.
Allow navigation from an article’s category label to articles.html with the appropriate categoryId parameter.
Provide a newsletter subscription form in the footer that calls createNewsletterSubscription and displays success or error feedback.
user_workflows:
View reading list:
1) User opens reading_list.html (optionally with groupBy and sortBy URL parameters).
2) Page reads groupBy and sortBy from the URL (or uses default logic if absent).
3) Page calls getReadingListItems with these parameters.
4) Page displays either grouped or ungrouped items based on the response.
5) User can scan article titles, summaries, and metadata.

Open an article from the reading list:
1) User identifies a saved article in the list.
2) User clicks the article title in the corresponding row.
3) Browser navigates to article_detail.html with articleId set to the clicked article’s id.
4) When the user returns to reading_list.html, the list is reloaded using the current URL parameters.

Remove an article from the reading list:
1) User locates an article row to remove.
2) User clicks the remove action associated with that row.
3) Page calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the articleId from that row.
4) If the response indicates success, the page updates the reading list: either re-calls getReadingListItems with the current groupBy and sortBy or removes the item from the in-memory list state and updates total_items using the returned value.
5) If the response indicates failure, an inline error message is shown in the list component.

Change grouping or sorting of the reading list:
1) User opens the grouping/sorting controls.
2) User selects a grouping option (e.g., grouped vs not grouped) and/or a sorting option.
3) Component updates the URL’s groupBy and sortBy query parameters without changing the base path (reading_list.html).
4) The change in URL triggers a new call to getReadingListItems with the selected groupBy and sortBy.
5) The reading list view updates to reflect the new grouping and sorting.

Browse articles by category from the reading list:
1) User locates an article row and its category label.
2) User clicks the category label.
3) Browser navigates to articles.html with categoryId set to the clicked article’s category field.
4) The articles page then handles displaying further content (outside the scope of this page).

Search from the header while on the reading list:
1) User types a query into the header search field and optionally selects a category filter.
2) User submits the search form.
3) Browser navigates to search_results.html with query, category, page (set to 1), and pageSize (default) as URL parameters.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the footer:
1) User scrolls to the footer newsletter form.
2) User fills in required fields (name, email) and selects topic checkboxes, frequency, and email format.
3) User submits the form.
4) Page calls createNewsletterSubscription with the form values.
5) On success, the component shows a success message and may clear or lock the form; on failure, it shows an error message while preserving user input.
interactions: Initial page load: On DOM ready, the page reads groupBy and sortBy from the URL query string and invokes getReadingListItems with those values.

Grouping control change: When the user selects a different grouping option, the control updates the groupBy URL parameter, preserves the current sortBy value, pushes the new state into the browser history, and triggers a refresh via getReadingListItems.

Sorting control change: When the user selects a different sort order, the control updates the sortBy URL parameter, preserves the current groupBy value, pushes the new state into the browser history, and triggers a refresh via getReadingListItems.
Click article title: A click handler on the article title takes the associated article.id and navigates to article_detail.html?articleId=<article.id>.
Click article category label: A click handler on the category label navigates to articles.html?categoryId=<article.category>.

Click remove from reading list: A click handler on the remove action calls removeArticleFromReadingList with article.id. Based on the response, the list component either removes the item from the displayed items or re-fetches all items from getReadingListItems.
Header navigation links: Click handlers on header items navigate to index.html, favorites.html, gallery.html, contact.html, or reading_list.html as appropriate.
Header search submit: The search form onSubmit event constructs a URL to search_results.html with query, category (if set), page=1, and pageSize=default, then navigates there.
Footer About link: A click handler on the About link navigates to about.html.

Footer newsletter submit: The newsletter form onSubmit event validates inputs and calls createNewsletterSubscription. It then shows either a success confirmation or an error message in the component state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is controlled through URL query parameters and API responses. On each load of reading_list.html, the page parses groupBy and sortBy from the query string. These values are passed directly into getReadingListItems.groupBy and getReadingListItems.sortBy. If either parameter is absent, the page applies internal default logic before calling getReadingListItems (e.g., a default grouping mode and default sort order) but does not hardcode any static article data. The response from getReadingListItems defines whether the view uses the flat items array or the grouped groups array; if group_by in the response indicates grouped data and groups is populated, the grouped view is used, otherwise the flat view uses items. When the user changes grouping or sorting via controls, the components update the groupBy and sortBy query parameters in the URL (using browser history APIs), then re-invoke getReadingListItems so the UI reflects the updated state. Removing an article via removeArticleFromReadingList does not update URL parameters; instead, the list state is updated either by re-fetching via getReadingListItems with the current URL parameters or by locally removing the item while updating total_items from the removeArticleFromReadingList response. Newsletter subscription state (success or error) is held inside the newsletter form component and is not reflected in URL parameters. No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used; all persistent reading list and subscription changes are managed exclusively through the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Provides global navigation links (Home, Reading list, Favorites, Photos, Contact) and a search form that routes to search_results.html.
event_handlers: onClickReadingListLink -> navigate to reading_list.html with existing query parameters preserved if desired

components.functionality: Initializes and refreshes the reading list data by parsing URL parameters and invoking getReadingListItems.
event_handlers: onPageLoad -> read groupBy and sortBy from URL query string, normalize defaults if missing, call getReadingListItems({ groupBy, sortBy }), and store the response in shared page state
onUrlChange (e.g., via history state) -> re-parse groupBy and sortBy from URL, call getReadingListItems with those values, and update shared page state
onRefreshRequested (e.g., after removeArticleFromReadingList) -> call getReadingListItems with the current groupBy and sortBy from shared state

components.functionality: Allows the user to change grouping and sorting options for the reading list and keeps the URL in sync with these options.
event_handlers: onGroupByChange(newGroupBy) -> update groupBy query parameter in URL while preserving sortBy; push new history state; trigger reading-list-state-loader.onUrlChange
onSortByChange(newSortBy) -> update sortBy query parameter in URL while preserving groupBy; push new history state; trigger reading-list-state-loader.onUrlChange

components.functionality: Displays a summary of the current reading list, including total items and the active grouping and sorting modes.
Renders reading list items grouped according to the groups array from getReadingListItems (for example, by article category) when grouping is active.
event_handlers: onClickGroupHeader(category_id) -> optional behavior, such as toggling group collapse/expand in local component state (no API call)

onClickRemoveFromReadingList(article.id) -> call removeArticleFromReadingList({ articleId: article.id }); on success, either remove the item from this component's state or trigger reading-list-state-loader.onRefreshRequested; on failure, display an inline error near the item

components.functionality: Renders reading list items as a single, ungrouped list based on the items array from getReadingListItems when grouping is not active or not supported.
event_handlers: onClickRemoveFromReadingList(article.id) -> call removeArticleFromReadingList({ articleId: article.id }); on success, either remove the item from this component's state or trigger reading-list-state-loader.onRefreshRequested; on failure, display an inline error near the item

components.functionality: Shows an informative message and navigation options when getReadingListItems.total_items is zero.
event_handlers: onClickBrowseArticles -> navigate to articles.html to let the user find new articles to save

components.functionality: Displays non-item-specific errors related to loading or updating the reading list, such as failures from getReadingListItems or removeArticleFromReadingList.
event_handlers: onDismissError -> clear errorState in page-level state

components.functionality: Collects user details and preferences to subscribe them to the newsletter using createNewsletterSubscription and provides feedback on the result.
event_handlers: onChangeField(fieldName, value) -> update local form state for the corresponding field

onSubmitNewsletterForm -> validate local form state; if valid, call createNewsletterSubscription with current form values; on success, show a success message and optionally reset or disable the form; on error, show an inline error message while preserving the user input

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a two-column layout: a narrow, fixed-width left column for the header-nav (logo + global navigation) and a wide, flexible right column for all reading-list-related components. The left column is a vertical stack that stops content roughly in the upper half of the viewport, leaving open space below. The right column behaves like an editorial canvas: a thin horizontal rule forms a quiet top edge, and all reading-list components stack vertically beneath it in a clear, single-column flow (error banner → state loader → controls → summary → list → empty state (when applicable) → footer newsletter). Only one of the main list representations (grouped or flat) is visible at a time, occupying the central area of the right column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a two-column layout: a narrow, fixed-width left column for the header-nav (logo + global navigation) and a wide, flexible right column for all reading-list-related components. The left column is a vertical stack that stops content roughly in the upper half of the viewport, leaving open space below. The right column behaves like an editorial canvas: a thin horizontal rule forms a quiet top edge, and all reading-list components stack vertically beneath it in a clear, single-column flow (error banner → state loader → controls → summary → list → empty state (when applicable) → footer newsletter). Only one of the main list representations (grouped or flat) is visible at a time, occupying the central area of the right column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Horizontally, the left navigation column occupies about 18–22% of the viewport width (roughly 1/5 of the screen), while the right content area takes the remaining 78–82%. The left column remains a fixed rail; the right column grows and scrolls vertically. Vertically, the logo and nav links occupy the top ~40–50% of the left column with generous gaps, leaving the lower half mostly empty. In the right column, the top horizontal rule sits roughly aligned with the bottom of the logo. Below that, major blocks (error banner, controls, summary, list, footer) are separated by 24–48px vertical spacing following an 8px rhythm, preserving large breathy gaps between functional groups.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low. Navigation items are spaced with roughly 16–24px vertical separation and minimal ornamentation. In the main content column, each functional group gets its own block with 24–32px internal padding and generous whitespace between them. List items in both grouped and flat views are vertically separated enough that each article feels like a discrete editorial entry, not a tight table. The loader, empty state, and footer newsletter are all surrounded by ample white space so no area feels crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Attention starts at the ornate green logo at the top-left, then moves straight down through the vertical navigation list. The eye then picks up the thin horizontal rule extending from the top of the right column, which leads gaze horizontally into the main content area. Immediately beneath that line, any error banner and the controls-panel form the functional 'header' for the reading list. From there, the eye travels downward through the summary bar and into the list (grouped or flat), which dominates the central vertical portion. Finally, the flow ends at the footer newsletter form near the bottom of the content column, framed by generous whitespace so it feels like a calm closing section rather than a busy utility bar.

layout.overall_description: The page is split into a left-hand vertical navigation rail and a broad, calm reading area on the right. On the left, the green plaque logo sits at the very top with comfortable top and left margins; beneath it, a vertical stack of uppercase navigation links runs down the left column with equal weight and generous spacing, including the Reading List link for the current page. A subtle caret next to one nav item hints at deeper sections but stays visually quiet. On the right, a thin horizontal rule runs across the top of the content column, just below the vertical position of the logo, subtly suggesting a header band. Below this line, the Reading List page content unfolds in a gentle vertical sequence: if there’s an error, a narrow banner appears first; the loader can temporarily occupy the central area while data is fetched. Once loaded, sorting and grouping controls sit in a compact panel near the top, followed by a concise summary bar, and then the main list view (either grouped or flat) occupies most of the vertical space. When the list is empty, a spacious, centered empty-state message replaces the list. At the very bottom of the right column, separated by a generous vertical gap from the list area, a simple, left-aligned newsletter signup form stretches partway across the content width, providing a soft, optional call-to-action that closes the page without disturbing its minimalist, editorial feel. The left rail remains visually anchored and largely static while the right content column scrolls, reinforcing the feeling of reading a personal journal rather than navigating a complex application.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-nav occupies the entire height of the left column as a fixed vertical sidebar, taking roughly 18–22% of the viewport width (for example, about 20% with a sensible minimum width to preserve legibility). It is aligned flush to the left edge of the viewport. At the very top, the ornate green logo sits with approximately 32px top margin and 24–32px left padding from the viewport edge, visually anchoring the column. Directly beneath the logo, after a vertical gap of around 32–40px, the navigation links are stacked in a single vertical column, each set in uppercase with equal visual weight. Each nav item has approximately 8–12px vertical padding and 8–16px horizontal padding, with about 16–24px vertical space between items, producing a slow, readable rhythm. The item representing the current page (e.g., 'READING LIST') appears in the same position in the stack as in the broader site, but may be subtly distinguished via weight or underline while still keeping the minimalist feel. One item (e.g., 'OUR RIDES') includes a small down-caret icon to its right, but the caret is compact and does not break the clean left-aligned text edge. The navigation block naturally fills the upper half of the sidebar, leaving the lower half as quiet, empty whitespace to preserve the airy editorial character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header-nav column is fixed in place or uses a sticky layout relative to the viewport (does not scroll with main content), maintaining a persistent left-hand rail while the right content column scrolls vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-state-loader appears in the right content column, not in the left nav. It occupies the central area beneath the top horizontal rule when data is loading. Horizontally, it is centered within the content column and constrained to a modest width (e.g., up to 320–400px) so it doesn’t feel dominant or heavy. Vertically, there is a gap of about 32–48px below the horizontal rule before the loader appears, and at least the same amount of whitespace below it, so it floats in a calm field of white. If an error banner is also present, the loader appears beneath it, with 24–32px separation. During loading, no list view or empty state is shown; the surrounding space in the right column remains mostly empty, reinforcing the site’s minimalist, contemplative tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader is a standard block element in the natural vertical flow of the right column content. It appears and disappears based on loading state without affecting the structural layout of the sidebar; it does not overlay or dim the entire page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-controls panel sits near the top of the right content column, directly under the top horizontal rule and any optional error banner or loader. It spans roughly 70–80% of the content column’s width, left-aligned so its left edge lines up with the start of the list items below, leaving a comfortable margin on the right for whitespace. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the horizontal rule (or the element above it) and this panel. Inside the panel, sorting and grouping controls (e.g., dropdowns, toggle buttons) are arranged on a single horizontal row, with subtle spacing between them (8–16px), preserving the light visual footprint. The panel itself has around 16–24px of internal padding and no heavy borders—perhaps only a very faint bottom line if needed—to maintain the minimalist, editorial feel. The overall height is relatively modest so that the controls feel like a light toolbar rather than a bulky header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The controls panel is part of the normal scrollable content. It remains static relative to the list (not sticky by default), moving off-screen as the user scrolls down through long reading lists.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-summary bar is positioned directly beneath the controls panel in the right column, separated from it by a vertical gap of around 16–24px. It is slightly narrower or equal in width to the controls panel, with its left edge aligned to the same vertical axis so the column feels orderly. The summary bar might display counts (e.g., total articles, unread items) and is visually lighter than the controls panel—perhaps a single line of text or a compact band with 12–16px top and bottom padding. It spans approximately 60–70% of the content width, leaving a generous right margin of empty space. This bar serves as a quiet, secondary header for the list, occupying a relatively shallow vertical slice before the main list content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The summary bar scrolls naturally with the reading list as part of the main content column, staying directly above whichever list view (grouped or flat) is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-grouped-view occupies the main central area of the right column when the user has chosen a grouped organization (e.g., by topic or date). It sits directly below the summary bar, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating them. Horizontally, the grouped view forms a single vertical stack of groups: each group header (e.g., a date or category label) is left-aligned with the summary bar and controls, spanning roughly 70–80% of the content width, with 16–24px top padding above each header to visually separate groups. The list items within each group are arranged in a simple, single column beneath their group header, each item occupying nearly the full width of that column with 16–24px vertical separation and 16–24px internal padding (like subtle cards but without heavy borders). The right side of the column remains open, preserving an airy margin. The grouped view can extend vertically and scroll, forming the main reading surface of the page. There is generous bottom padding (48–64px) below the last group before the footer newsletter form begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The grouped view is a scrollable block that fills the available height of the right column beneath the summary bar. Only one main list representation is visible at a time; when the grouped view is active, the flat view is hidden but occupies the same spatial slot in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-flat-view shares the same spatial slot as the grouped view and is displayed instead when the list is not grouped. It begins in the right column directly beneath the summary bar, with about 24–32px vertical spacing above it. The flat list is a single, vertical column of saved articles, left-aligned with the summary bar and controls and spanning approximately 70–80% of the content width. Each list item is separated by 16–24px of vertical space and has 16–24px of internal padding, giving each article the feel of a standalone entry on an editorial reading list rather than a cramped row. There are no heavy dividing lines; at most, a faint divider or subtle change in background for hover states. The list extends down the page and becomes the primary scrollable region within the content column, with at least 48–64px of padding at the bottom before reaching the footer newsletter section, maintaining the open, quiet feel of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Like the grouped view, the flat view is a scrollable block. It is mutually exclusive with the grouped view—only one is rendered at a time—occupying the same vertical position beneath the summary bar and above the footer newsletter form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-empty-state appears in place of either list view when the reading list contains no items. It is positioned in the right column under the summary bar (or directly under the controls panel if the summary is omitted when empty), with a larger vertical gap of about 32–48px above it to emphasize the emptiness of the space. The empty state content (icon, title, descriptive text, and possibly a link to discover articles) is contained within a centered block that occupies approximately 40–60% of the content width. The block is horizontally centered in the right column, but its text is left-aligned to keep the editorial feel. There is abundant white space: at least 48px of padding inside the empty state block and 64–96px of clear space below before the footer newsletter appears. The result is a calm, contemplative message floating in a mostly blank canvas, perfectly aligned with the minimalist, personal-blog tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state replaces the list views in the normal flow. It does not overlay other components; it simply occupies the main content area beneath the summary bar and scrolls with the page if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-error-banner is a slim, horizontal band that appears at the top of the right content column whenever an error occurs (e.g., loading or saving issues). It sits directly beneath the thin horizontal rule that defines the top of the content area, with a small vertical gap of about 16px between the rule and the banner so the rule remains visible. The banner spans nearly the full width of the right column (90–100% of the content width) but maintains left alignment consistent with the controls and list. It has a modest height, defined by about 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, and a background color that stands out enough to signal error while still respecting the understated palette. Below the banner, there is about 16–24px of whitespace before the next component (typically the loader or the controls panel), ensuring the message is noticeable but not visually overwhelming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The error banner is a non-sticky block in the natural vertical flow, appearing at the top of the reading-list content area. It scrolls away as the user moves down the list, preserving the tranquil reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-newsletter-form lives at the bottom of the right content column as a closing section to the Reading List page. It appears after the list views (grouped or flat) or the empty state, separated from the preceding content by a generous vertical gap of 48–64px to make it feel like a separate, optional section. The form is left-aligned with the main content column: its left edge lines up with the list items and controls, but its width is constrained to around 50–60% of the content column or a comfortable max width (e.g., 480–600px), leaving a wide margin of white space to the right. The elements inside (headline, short description, email field, submit button) stack vertically with 8–16px spacing between them, and the entire form has about 24–32px of internal padding. There is ample bottom padding (48–64px) below the form before the end of the page to avoid a cramped footer edge, maintaining the overall airy, editorial feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The newsletter form is part of the normal scrollable flow and sits at the bottom of the right column’s content. It does not stick to the viewport edge; users encounter it naturally once they reach the end of their reading list.

name: Favorites
title: Favorites
description: Unified favorites page that shows all favorited articles and photos, lets users manage favorites, and provides a simple photo viewer controlled by URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display all favorited articles and photos using getFavoritesOverview, grouped into an Articles section and a Photos section.
Support a view filter (all, articles, photos) via the view URL parameter to focus the relevant favorites section.
Allow navigation from favorited article titles to their Article Detail pages and from article category labels to the Articles listing for that category.
Display favorited photos as thumbnails; clicking a thumbnail opens a large photo viewer (lightbox) controlled by the photoId URL parameter with next/previous navigation within favorites.
Allow removing articles and photos from favorites directly on this page using removeArticleFromFavorites and removePhotoFromFavorites, updating counts and lists without hardcoded values.
Provide a footer newsletter subscription form that creates a NewsletterSubscription via createNewsletterSubscription.
user_workflows: Viewing all favorites: 1) User opens favorites.html with no URL parameters. 2) Page controller calls getFavoritesOverview. 3) Page shows Articles and Photos sections with counts and the 'all' view active by default. 4) User can scroll both sections and interact with items.

Focusing on articles or photos: 1) User clicks the 'Articles' or 'Photos' tab. 2) The page navigates to favorites.html?view=articles or favorites.html?view=photos. 3) On load/URL change, the controller reads view and sets the corresponding section as primary (e.g., scrolled into view, visually emphasized) while still allowing access to both sections.

Opening a favorited article: 1) User finds an article in the Favorites article list. 2) User clicks the article title. 3) Browser navigates to article_detail.html?articleId=<Article.id>. 4) The user can read the full article and later return to favorites.html via navigation.

Browsing favorited photos and opening the viewer: 1) User scrolls to the Photos section (or uses the Photos tab). 2) Favorited photos are rendered as thumbnails based on getFavoritesOverview.photos. 3) User clicks a thumbnail. 4) Browser navigates to favorites.html?photoId=<Photo.id> (and optionally preserves view). 5) On load/URL change, the photo viewer opens showing that photo in large format.

Navigating between photos in the viewer: 1) With the viewer open (photoId present), user clicks 'Next' or 'Previous'. 2) Page computes the next/previous photo from the ordered favorites array. 3) Browser updates the URL to favorites.html?photoId=<nextPhotoId> (and optionally view). 4) Controller responds to URL change and updates the viewer to show the new photo without requiring a new getFavoritesOverview call.

Closing the photo viewer: 1) User clicks the close button, overlay, or presses Escape. 2) The page updates the URL to remove the photoId parameter (keeping view if present). 3) Controller detects URL change and hides the photo viewer while keeping the Favorites page visible at the same scroll position.

Removing an article from favorites: 1) User clicks a 'Remove from favorites' control on an article item. 2) Component calls removeArticleFromFavorites with the corresponding articleId. 3) On success, the article is removed from the local in-memory favorites list and the articles total is updated using the response.total_favorites or recalculated. 4) If there are no remaining favorited articles, the Articles section shows an empty-state message driven by dynamic data (e.g., total_favorite_articles).

Removing a photo from favorites (from grid): 1) User clicks a 'Remove from favorites' control on a photo thumbnail. 2) Component calls removePhotoFromFavorites with photoId. 3) On success, the photo is removed from the local favorites photos array, total_favorite_photos is updated from the response or recalculated, and the Photos section re-renders. 4) If the viewer is open for this photo (photoId in URL), the viewer logic responds as if the current photo was removed and navigates to another available photo or closes.

Removing a photo from favorites (from viewer): 1) With the viewer open, user clicks 'Remove from favorites' in the viewer. 2) Component calls removePhotoFromFavorites with the current photoId. 3) On success, the current photo is removed from the local favorites array. 4) Viewer determines whether a next or previous photo still exists; if yes, URL is updated to that photoId; if no, photoId is removed from URL and the viewer closes.

Subscribing to the newsletter: 1) User scrolls to the footer and fills in the newsletter form fields (name, email, topic checkboxes, frequency, email format). 2) User submits the form. 3) Component calls createNewsletterSubscription with the entered values. 4) On success, the component shows a success message and may clear or lock the form; on failure, it shows an error message without hardcoded text about specific causes.

Using header navigation and search: 1) User uses header links (Home, Photos, Contact, Favorites, etc.) to navigate to other pages using standard anchor links and URL parameters where needed. 2) If user submits the header search form, the browser navigates to search_results.html with query, category, page=1, and pageSize set in the query string.
interactions: Clicking view tabs sets the view URL parameter and triggers re-evaluation of which section is emphasized.
Clicking an article title navigates to article_detail.html with the articleId parameter derived from the Article.id.
Clicking a category label on an article card navigates to articles.html with categoryId set to the article's category.
Clicking a photo thumbnail navigates to favorites.html with photoId set to the photo's id (and optionally view preserved) which opens the photo viewer.
Clicking next/previous in the photo viewer updates the URL's photoId to the adjacent favorited photo and updates the displayed photo accordingly.
Clicking the close action in the photo viewer removes photoId from the URL, closing the viewer without reloading the Favorites data.
Clicking 'Remove from favorites' on an article calls removeArticleFromFavorites(articleId) and updates the UI based on the success flag and returned total_favorites.
Clicking 'Remove from favorites' on a photo (in grid or viewer) calls removePhotoFromFavorites(photoId) and updates the UI based on the success flag and returned total_favorite_photos.
Submitting the newsletter form calls createNewsletterSubscription with the current form values, then displays success or error feedback driven by the response.
Submitting the header search form triggers a navigation to search_results.html with appropriate query string parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load and on any URL change, the page controller reads the view and photoId URL parameters. If view is not present or not recognized, it defaults to an internal 'all' state. If view=articles, the Articles section is emphasized (for example, scrolled into view or expanded) while Photos remains accessible; if view=photos, the Photos section is emphasized. Regardless of view, both favorite articles and photos are fetched once using getFavoritesOverview and cached in client memory. The photoId parameter controls the photo viewer state: if photoId is present and matches a Photo.id in the favorites photos list, the viewer opens with that photo selected; if photoId is absent or does not match any current favorite, the viewer remains closed. Navigation within the viewer updates the photoId parameter in the URL to the next or previous favorite photo without re-calling getFavoritesOverview. Closing the viewer removes photoId from the URL while preserving the current view parameter. When items are removed from favorites via removeArticleFromFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites, the local in-memory lists and counts are updated in sync with the API response; if a removed item was referenced in the current URL state (e.g., the active photoId), the controller adjusts the URL and derived state (viewer open/closed, selected photo) accordingly.

components.functionality: Initializes the Favorites page, calls getFavoritesOverview, parses URL parameters (view, photoId), manages in-memory state for favorited articles and photos, and coordinates updates to child components when the URL or favorites data change.
Displays controls (e.g., tabs or buttons) to switch between 'All', 'Articles', and 'Photos' views, using the total favorite counts for labels and updating the view URL parameter.
Shows a concise summary of how many articles and photos are currently in favorites using totals from getFavoritesOverview and updates when items are removed.
Wraps the list of favorited articles, handling emphasis or de-emphasis based on the current view state and showing dynamic empty-state messaging when there are no favorited articles.

Renders each favorited article as an item with title, category label, optional tag labels, and metadata from Article plus favorite-added information from FavoriteArticle. Provides navigation to article_detail and category-based article lists, and allows removing an article from favorites.
Displays a message or hint when there are zero favorited articles, based solely on the dynamic count from getFavoritesOverview or updated totals after removals.
Wraps the grid of favorited photos, integrates with the view state to emphasize or de-emphasize the section, and shows a dynamic empty-state message when there are no favorited photos.

Displays favorited photos as thumbnails taken from Photo.thumbnail_url with ordering based on FavoritePhoto.added_at or another defined ordering. Each thumbnail supports opening the photo viewer and removing the photo from favorites.
Shows a message or hint when there are no favorited photos, determined from total_favorite_photos or the length of the favorites photos array.

Implements a lightbox-style photo viewer that opens when the photoId URL parameter is present and matches a favorited photo. Displays Photo.image_url, optional title, description, and related metadata. Provides next/previous navigation within the current favorites set, supports closing the viewer, and allows removing the currently viewed photo from favorites while updating the URL state.
Provides site-wide navigation links (Home, Articles, Photos, Favorites, Contact) and includes a header search form that navigates to the Search Results page with appropriate query parameters.

Collects newsletter subscription details (name, email, topic preferences, frequency, and email format) and submits them using createNewsletterSubscription, showing success or error states based on the response.

Displays transient success or error messages related to favorite add/remove actions and newsletter subscription, using messages returned from removeArticleFromFavorites, removePhotoFromFavorites, and createNewsletterSubscription.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a two-column layout: a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar for logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, fluid right content area for the Favorites page. In the right area, content is stacked in a clean vertical flow: top horizontal rule, then page-level controls (view toggle + summary + notifications), then the articles section, then the photos section, and finally the newsletter form at the bottom. The photo viewer appears as a modal overlay centered over the entire page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a two-column layout: a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar for logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, fluid right content area for the Favorites page. In the right area, content is stacked in a clean vertical flow: top horizontal rule, then page-level controls (view toggle + summary + notifications), then the articles section, then the photos section, and finally the newsletter form at the bottom. The photo viewer appears as a modal overlay centered over the entire page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, allocate roughly 18–22% of viewport width (min ~240px, max ~320px) to the left sidebar and 78–82% to the right content area. Vertically, keep the logo and navigation clustered in the upper-left of the sidebar, using about the top half of the column and leaving the lower half mostly empty. In the main content area, maintain a slim top band with a horizontal rule near the top, then use generous vertical spacing (32–48px) between the major blocks: page controls, articles section, photos section, and footer form.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a very low content density overall. Navigation items are spaced with generous vertical padding (around 8–12px per item with 16–24px gaps between groups). In the main content, each section breathes: 24–32px internal padding per section, 24px spacing between list items or grid rows, and substantial marginal whitespace to the right of text columns. The design should feel calm and uncluttered, with no element crowding another.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the ornate green logo at the top-left (outside this component set but part of the global layout), then moves down the vertical navigation (including the Favorites link). The horizontal rule along the top of the right column then pulls the eye across into the content area. From there, the primary reading path is: view toggle tabs, then the favorites summary, then down into the articles section, then further down into the photos section, and finally to the quiet newsletter form at the bottom. When opened, the photo viewer modal temporarily hijacks the visual flow, centering attention on the enlarged photo and its controls.

layout.overall_description: The page is split into a left vertical navigation rail and a large, mostly blank canvas on the right. In the left rail, the decorative green plaque logo sits near the top-left corner, with ample white space around it. Directly beneath, a vertical stack of navigation links—including a Favorites link—runs down the sidebar, each all-caps with equal visual weight and generous vertical spacing. A subtle caret sits beside any expandable item, echoing the crafted feel of the mockup. The right-hand content area begins with a thin horizontal rule stretching across near the top, creating a quiet header band with plenty of empty space above and below it. Below this rule, the Favorites content is arranged as a relaxed vertical column: first the view-toggle tabs to switch between combined/filtered favorites views, then a concise summary of how many articles and photos are favorited, followed by any notification messages. The favorite articles section appears next, occupying a comfortable text column width on the left side of the right pane, with individual article rows stacked and separated by airy gaps or, when empty, a light, centered message. Further down, the favorite photos section follows with a simple, evenly spaced grid of thumbnails aligned under the same left margin, leaving open white space to the right. At the very bottom of the scroll, a minimalist newsletter form sits quietly as a soft footer element. When the user opens a favorited photo, a modal viewer gently dims the rest of the page and centers the selected image, preserving the minimalist aesthetic while briefly taking over the entire visual field.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overall two-column canvas. It positions a fixed-height viewport that is split horizontally into two main regions: a left sidebar column and a right main content area. The left sidebar sits flush against the left edge of the viewport, occupies approximately 20% of the width (with a minimum width of about 240px and a maximum of about 320px), and stretches from the top to the bottom of the viewport. The right main content area begins immediately to the right of this sidebar, fills the remaining 80% of the width, and extends vertically as long as the content requires, with the page scrolling vertically. Within the right area, the controller reserves a slim top band—about 48–64px tall—where the thin horizontal rule runs close to the top, aligning horizontally with the vertical center of the logo in the left column. All favorites-related components (view toggle, summary, notifications, sections, newsletter form) are stacked in a single vertical column within this right region, aligned to a consistent left margin (around 64px from the inside edge of the sidebar) to preserve a strong editorial column feel. Lateral whitespace to the right of this content column remains mostly empty, emphasizing the sparse, content-first aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a fixed two-column flex layout: left sidebar is fixed (or sticky) relative to the viewport height; the right content column scrolls vertically while the sidebar remains visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation sits in the left sidebar, directly below the site logo. It forms a vertical list of navigation items aligned to the left edge of the sidebar. The first nav item begins approximately 24–32px beneath the bottom of the logo, with subsequent items stacked downward. Each item is a single line in all caps, with about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the label text and 0–16px horizontal padding to give a slightly generous clickable area while still feeling lean. Items are left-aligned, with their left edges forming a straight vertical line. The 'OUR RIDES' item includes a small down-caret icon positioned immediately to the right of its text, maintaining the same baseline as the label. The current page ('FAVORITES') is indicated subtly (e.g., by a lighter underline or a slightly darker text tone), but not with strong background blocks, preserving the minimalist feel. The entire navigation block occupies roughly the upper half of the sidebar’s height, leaving plenty of empty vertical space below the last link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the fixed/sticky left sidebar column; remains in place as users scroll the right-hand content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-toggle tabs are placed near the top of the right content area, just below the thin horizontal rule. Starting from the inner edge of the right column, there is approximately 32px of vertical space between the rule and the top of the tabs. The tabs are arranged left-to-right as a small cluster, occupying the left third of the right content width, and they sit on a single line. Each tab label (e.g., ‘ALL’, ‘ARTICLES’, ‘PHOTOS’) has moderate horizontal padding (16–24px) and minimal vertical height, with subtle underline or weight changes to indicate the active state instead of heavy button chrome. The tabs align their left edge with the main content column (approximately 64px from the inside edge of the sidebar), reinforcing the editorial column feel. There is open whitespace to the right of the tabs, leaving the majority of the upper right portion of the content area blank, which keeps the interface feeling calm and unbusy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content; visually anchored near the top of the right content column but not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites summary sits immediately below the view-toggle tabs within the same right-hand column. There is approximately 8–16px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the tabs and the top of the summary text, keeping them visually grouped but distinct. The summary is a short, single or double-line text block (e.g., ‘12 favorite articles · 34 favorite photos’) aligned to the same left margin as the tabs. It occupies no more than half the width of the content column, allowing the text to sit in a compact line or two with ample whitespace to the right. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of space from the bottom of the summary to the next element (typically the notifications or the header of the articles section), maintaining the low-density aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content, stacked directly under the view-toggle tabs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Notification messages appear as a slim, subtle bar below the favorites summary, used sparingly to confirm actions such as removing a favorite or saving changes. The notification bar spans the same effective width as the main content column but is slightly inset: it starts at the same left margin as the summary and ends roughly 16–24px before the right edge of the content column to avoid feeling full-bleed. Vertically, it sits about 8–16px below the summary and about 24–32px above the start of the first content section. The notification text is left-aligned, single-line where possible, with modest padding (8–12px top and bottom, 16px left and right) inside a light, low-contrast background to avoid breaking the minimalist feel. When no notifications are active, this space collapses fully, allowing the sections above and below to move closer together without leaving a visible gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the scrolling content, occupying temporary vertical space beneath the summary; it does not overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite articles section is the first major content block below the page controls. It begins roughly 32–40px beneath the notifications (or beneath the summary if there are no notifications), maintaining generous separation. The section title (e.g., ‘Favorite Articles’) is aligned to the main content left margin, forming the start of a vertical text column. Below the title, any section-level controls (like ‘Remove all’) are placed to the right of the title on the same baseline or directly beneath it, but kept minimal and light. The entire section is visually confined to a readable content column of approximately 640–720px in width, aligned to the left of the right panel, leaving a wide, unused whitespace band on the right side of the page. The section has internal vertical padding of about 24px at the top and bottom so that its contents (list or empty state) feel comfortably nested within. There is a generous gap (about 48–64px) between the bottom of this section and the start of the photos section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content; behaves as a standard block-level container that expands vertically as its list grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the favorite articles section, the articles list is arranged as a single vertical stack of article entries. The list starts approximately 16–24px below the section heading, aligned to the same left margin and constrained by the 640–720px content width. Each article entry spans the full width of this column and includes its title, date, and any small actions (e.g., ‘Remove from favorites’) arranged vertically or in a subtle horizontal row. Entries are separated by consistent vertical spacing—around 24px of space between each item’s bounding box—to preserve the airy editorial rhythm. There is no visible border around the list as a whole; if item-level separation is needed, use hairline dividers or light tonal differences that avoid disrupting the minimalist layout. The bottom of the list stops well before the right edge of the screen, leaving the right side of the main content area mostly empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically as more articles are favorited; remains within the bounds of the favorite-articles-section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no favorited articles, the articles list is replaced by an empty-state message inside the favorite articles section. This empty-state block is positioned where the list would begin: about 16–24px below the section title, aligned to the main content left margin. The message itself is a compact text block (possibly with a very small icon) centered horizontally within the section’s text column but left-aligned in terms of text alignment, keeping with the editorial look. It may be surrounded by subtle vertical padding (24–32px above and below) to create a soft island of content within an otherwise blank area. The right side of the page remains empty, emphasizing the sense of openness and gently nudging the user toward exploring content without feeling pushy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same spatial slot as the list, expanding the section just enough to convey the message; collapses completely when there are articles to show.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite photos section sits below the favorite articles section, separated by a large vertical gap of approximately 48–64px to clearly delineate the two content types. Its header (e.g., ‘Favorite Photos’) is aligned to the same left margin as the articles header, maintaining a consistent vertical column. The internal padding mirrors that of the articles section: about 24px at the top and bottom. The photos section content (grid or empty state) starts about 16–24px below the section title. While the text aligns with the 640–720px text column, the grid itself may extend slightly wider if needed but should still respect at least 24px of inner right padding to avoid a cramped feeling against the edge of the viewport. The lower portion of the section transitions smoothly into the newsletter form area, with a comfortable vertical gap below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls as part of the main content; expands vertically to accommodate the grid or the empty state copy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the photos section, the favorite photos grid displays as a simple, evenly spaced grid of thumbnails. It begins roughly 16–24px below the photos section title, aligned to the same left margin. On wider screens, the grid uses 3–4 columns depending on available width, with each thumbnail square or slightly rectangular and separated from neighbors by consistent gaps of about 16px (horizontal and vertical). The grid occupies a central band within the right content area: its left edge is aligned with the main text column, and its right edge remains at least 24px from the right side of the viewport, leaving a slender vertical strip of whitespace. There is no heavy framing; each image sits on the white background, optionally with a fine border or subtle shadow only on hover to keep the default state minimal. The overall grid height grows as more rows are added, and the bottom of the grid leaves about 32–40px before any subsequent elements (like the newsletter form).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grows vertically as more photos are favorited; retains a responsive column count while staying within the photos section’s constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If there are no favorite photos, the grid area is replaced with an empty-state message. This message appears in place of the grid, starting around 16–24px below the photos section title and aligned with the same left margin. The copy is concise and may include a very small, line-style illustration of a horse or camera to keep the page personable. The message block is contained within the text column width (640–720px) and is given extra vertical breathing room—about 24–32px above and below—so that, even in the absence of images, the section still feels deliberate rather than empty. The rest of the right content area remains largely blank, highlighting the minimal nature of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the grid’s intended space; the section’s height adjusts to the size of the message and then transitions to the elements below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites photo viewer appears as a modal overlay that sits on top of the entire page when a photo is opened. It covers the full viewport with a semi-transparent, low-contrast dark scrim that gently dims the sidebar and main content while keeping their shapes faintly visible. At the center of the viewport, a content panel displays the selected photo. This panel takes up roughly 70% of the viewport width and 70–80% of its height, staying within comfortable margins on all sides (at least 48–64px from each edge on large screens). The photo is centered within this panel, with minimal chrome: perhaps simple left/right arrow controls at the horizontal midline, and a small close icon at the top-right corner of the panel. Any caption or metadata sits in a narrow band beneath the photo, aligned left, using the same quiet typography as the rest of the site. The modal’s presence temporarily dominates the visual flow, but the overall composition remains clean and uncluttered, with no elements touching the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay with a high z-index that sits above both sidebar and main content; scroll is locked behind the modal while it is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter form functions as a gentle footer element at the bottom of the right content column. It is positioned approximately 48–64px below the end of the favorite photos section (or its empty state), maintaining a sense of separation from the main favorites content. The form is constrained to the same text column width as the articles and section headings (about 640–720px), aligned to the left margin, and does not extend to the full width of the right content area. It typically includes a small, understated heading (e.g., ‘Stay in the pasture with us’) and a single-line email input with a subtle submit button aligned inline. Vertical padding around the form is modest but deliberate—about 24px above the first form element and 24–32px below the last—to give it presence without overwhelming the page. The overall look feels like a quiet invitation rather than a pushy call to action, with plenty of white space above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the bottom of the main content stack; scrolls normally and expands slightly if validation messages appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites photo viewer appears as a modal overlay that sits on top of the entire page when a photo is opened. It covers the full viewport with a semi-transparent, low-contrast dark scrim that gently dims the sidebar and main content while keeping their shapes faintly visible. At the center of the viewport, a content panel displays the selected photo. This panel takes up roughly 70% of the viewport width and 70–80% of its height, staying within comfortable margins on all sides (at least 48–64px from each edge on large screens). The photo is centered within this panel, with minimal chrome: perhaps simple left/right arrow controls at the horizontal midline, and a small close icon at the top-right corner of the panel. Any caption or metadata sits in a narrow band beneath the photo, aligned left, using the same quiet typography as the rest of the site. The modal’s presence temporarily dominates the visual flow, but the overall composition remains clean and uncluttered, with no elements touching the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay with a high z-index that sits above both sidebar and main content; scroll is locked behind the modal while it is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-toggle tabs are placed near the top of the right content area, just below the thin horizontal rule. Starting from the inner edge of the right column, there is approximately 32px of vertical space between the rule and the top of the tabs. The tabs are arranged left-to-right as a small cluster, occupying the left third of the right content width, and they sit on a single line. Each tab label (e.g., ‘ALL’, ‘ARTICLES’, ‘PHOTOS’) has moderate horizontal padding (16–24px) and minimal vertical height, with subtle underline or weight changes to indicate the active state instead of heavy button chrome. The tabs align their left edge with the main content column (approximately 64px from the inside edge of the sidebar), reinforcing the editorial column feel. There is open whitespace to the right of the tabs, leaving the majority of the upper right portion of the content area blank, which keeps the interface feeling calm and unbusy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content; visually anchored near the top of the right content column but not fixed or sticky.

name: Photo Gallery
title: Photo Gallery
description: Page for browsing horse photos with year and category filters, favoriting photos, viewing larger versions, navigating to the Favorites page, and subscribing to the newsletter.

core_features: Load available year and category filters for the gallery using getPhotoGalleryFilters and render them as filter controls that update URL parameters.
Retrieve and display paginated gallery photos using getGalleryPhotos, applying year, category, and page URL parameters to determine which photos are shown.

Allow users to mark or unmark individual photos as favorites directly from thumbnails or the large viewer using addPhotoToFavorites and removePhotoFromFavorites, updating the favorite state per photo.
Provide navigation to a dedicated Favorites page via a My favorites link or header Favorites navigation item, where only favorited photos are shown (navigation only, no additional interfaces).
Allow users to click a thumbnail to open a larger in-page or overlay viewer for closer inspection, reusing the data from getGalleryPhotos and keeping favorite state consistent.
Provide contextual navigation from relevant gallery categories (for example training or competitions) to related articles on articles.html by constructing URLs with a categoryId query parameter.
Offer a newsletter subscription form in the footer that creates a newsletter subscription using createNewsletterSubscription with user-entered preferences.
user_workflows: Browse photos with filters: (1) User arrives on gallery.html, optionally with year, category, and page URL parameters. (2) Page controller calls getPhotoGalleryFilters to load available year_filters and category_filters. (3) Page controller reads URL parameters and calls getGalleryPhotos with the corresponding year, category, page, and a configured pageSize. (4) The gallery grid displays thumbnails based on the returned photos and reflects the applied_filters in the filter controls. (5) User scrolls through the grid and uses pagination to move between pages; each pagination action updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers a new getGalleryPhotos call.

Change year or category: (1) User selects a different year from the year filter or selects a different category tab. (2) Filter bar updates the year or category URL parameter and sets page to 1 in the navigation to gallery.html. (3) Page reload or routing event triggers the page controller to re-read the URL parameters and call getGalleryPhotos with the new filters. (4) Grid is refreshed with photos for the chosen year and category, and filter controls highlight the selected options.

Mark and unmark favorite photos: (1) User clicks the favorite icon on a photo thumbnail. (2) If the photo is not yet favorited (is_favorited is false), the gallery grid component calls addPhotoToFavorites with the photoId. (3) On a successful response, the component updates that photo's favorite state in memory to true and may update any displayed favorite count using total_favorite_photos. (4) If the user clicks the favorite icon again on a favorited photo, the grid calls removePhotoFromFavorites with the same photoId. (5) On a successful response, the component updates that photo's favorite state to false and adjusts any displayed favorite count using total_favorite_photos, without reloading the entire page.

View larger photo details: (1) User clicks on a thumbnail image (not on the favorite icon). (2) Gallery grid opens a lightbox or in-page large viewer component, passing the corresponding Photo data and its is_favorited state. (3) Within the viewer, the user can navigate to the previous or next photo currently loaded in the grid, and the viewer updates the displayed Photo without additional API calls. (4) The user can toggle the favorite state from within the viewer; this calls addPhotoToFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites just like the thumbnail and updates both the viewer and underlying grid state. (5) User closes the viewer to return to the grid.

Navigate to Favorites and related stories: (1) User clicks a My favorites link or header Favorites navigation element. (2) Browser navigates to favorites.html with no additional parameters; the favorites page handles its own data loading. (3) When the user is viewing a category in the gallery such as training or competitions, contextual links are shown. (4) Clicking a contextual link navigates to articles.html with a categoryId query parameter based on the active gallery category (only when applicable), without calling additional interfaces from this page.

Use header search and basic navigation: (1) User types a search term into the header search input and submits. (2) The page constructs a navigation to search_results.html with query, page set to 1, and pageSize set to a default defined outside this page; category may optionally be included based on the search UI. (3) User can also navigate via header to Home (index.html) or Contact (contact.html), and via footer to About (about.html), by simple link navigation without invoking page-specific interfaces.

Subscribe to newsletter from the gallery page: (1) User fills in the newsletter form in the footer with name, email, topic preferences (for example wants_training_tips, wants_competition_updates, wants_merchandise_offers), frequency, and email_format. (2) On submit, the footer component calls createNewsletterSubscription with these values. (3) On success, the component may show a confirmation message and clear the form fields. (4) On failure, the component shows an error message derived from the returned message and allows resubmission without leaving the gallery page.
interactions: Year filter interaction: The year dropdown or buttons are populated from getPhotoGalleryFilters.year_filters. Selecting a year updates the year parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, keeps the current category (if any), and navigates to gallery.html with the new parameters, which triggers a fresh getGalleryPhotos call.

Category tab interaction: Category tabs are populated from getPhotoGalleryFilters.category_filters. Selecting a category updates the category parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, keeps the current year (if any), and navigates to gallery.html, which leads to a new getGalleryPhotos call with the chosen category.

Pagination interaction: Pagination controls use getGalleryPhotos.page, getGalleryPhotos.page_size, and getGalleryPhotos.total_results to calculate available pages. When the user selects a page number or next/previous, the component updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving year and category, then navigates to gallery.html so that getGalleryPhotos is called with the new page.

Thumbnail hover and click: Hovering over a thumbnail may reveal the favorite control if it is not always visible (behavior determined by UI). Clicking a thumbnail (excluding the favorite control) opens the large viewer with that photo's details and current is_favorited value; no additional API calls are made at this moment.

Favorite icon on thumbnails: The favorite icon reflects the is_favorited flag returned in getGalleryPhotos for each photo. Clicking the icon triggers either addPhotoToFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites based on the current state. The icon state and any displayed favorite count are updated immediately using the response data without reloading the gallery.

Favorite toggle in large viewer: The large viewer shows a favorite control bound to the same is_favorited state as the underlying thumbnail. Clicking it calls addPhotoToFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites, updates the viewer's UI, and also updates the corresponding photo entry in the grid so that when the viewer closes, the grid shows the correct state.

My favorites navigation: A My favorites link within the gallery area or a Favorites item in the header, when clicked, navigates to favorites.html without additional parameters. This interaction does not affect the current gallery filters but moves the user to the favorites section of the site.

Contextual article link interaction: When the active gallery category corresponds to an article category supported for navigation (such as training or competitions), a contextual link appears. Clicking this link navigates to articles.html with categoryId set to the matching category value, allowing cross-navigation without calling any interfaces from this page.
