Header search interaction: User submits the header search form, which constructs a URL to search_results.html with query taken from the user input, page set to 1, and pageSize set according to a default configuration; category may optionally be included based on additional UI controls. The gallery page does not call any search-related interfaces.

Newsletter form interaction: User interacts with the newsletter fields (text inputs, checkboxes, selects). On form submission, default browser submission is prevented, and createNewsletterSubscription is called with the current form values. The component handles success and error states based on the returned success and message fields and may disable the submit button during the request.
page_functionality.state_logic: The gallery's primary state (selected year, selected category, and current page number) is represented solely through URL query parameters year, category, and page on gallery.html. On initial page load or when the URL changes due to navigation, the page controller reads these parameters and uses them as follows: (1) year: If present, its numeric value is passed as the year argument to getGalleryPhotos. If absent, the year argument is omitted or passed as an undefined value, allowing the backend to apply its default behavior (such as showing all years or a default year). (2) category: If present, its string value is passed as the category argument to getGalleryPhotos and is used to activate the corresponding category tab in the filter bar. If absent, the category argument is omitted or passed as an undefined value; the UI can either show a neutral 'all categories' state or highlight a default category determined by the front-end logic without hardcoding any specific category id. (3) page: If present, its numeric value is passed as the page argument to getGalleryPhotos; if absent, page defaults to the first page in the front-end logic (for example by treating a missing page parameter as page 1). The pageSize argument for getGalleryPhotos is supplied from configuration or component input rather than being hardcoded in this specification, ensuring flexibility. After calling getGalleryPhotos, the page uses the applied_filters returned by the interface as the source of truth for which filters are actually in effect, which may differ from the raw URL values if the backend adjusts or normalizes them. Whenever the user changes the year or category through the filter bar, the front-end constructs a new URL for gallery.html that includes the chosen year and category and sets page to 1 to avoid requesting an out-of-range page; navigation to this URL triggers the standard reload or router transition and a new getGalleryPhotos call. When the user changes pagination, only the page parameter is updated while keeping year and category unchanged. Favorite state is not encoded in the URL; instead, each photo's favorite status is provided by getGalleryPhotos via the is_favorited flag and is updated in memory upon addPhotoToFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites responses. Any displayed total favorite photo count is derived from the total_favorite_photos field returned by these favorite interfaces and maintained in page-level state, independent of URL parameters. Newsletter subscription state is not stored in the URL; instead, it is transient form state within the footer component, and successful submissions are confirmed using the createNewsletterSubscription response.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and response to URL changes for the photo gallery page. On load, reads URL parameters (year, category, page), calls getPhotoGalleryFilters to obtain available filter options, then calls getGalleryPhotos with the derived year, category, page, and a configured pageSize. Distributes the resulting data to child components such as the filter bar, photo grid, and pagination. Listens for internal navigation intents (for example filter changes or page changes) and updates the URL accordingly, triggering reloads or route changes as required.

Displays year and category controls for filtering the photo gallery. Year options are generated from getPhotoGalleryFilters.year_filters and category tabs from getPhotoGalleryFilters.category_filters. Highlights the active year and category based on applied_filters returned from getGalleryPhotos or the current URL parameters. When the user selects a different year or category, the component constructs a new gallery.html URL with updated year and/or category parameters, sets page to 1, and initiates navigation so that the page controller can reload photos with the new filters.

Renders a grid of photo thumbnails using the photos array from getGalleryPhotos. Each thumbnail shows the photo thumbnail_url, optional title or caption if provided, and a favorite icon whose state is determined by the is_favorited flag for that photo. Clicking a thumbnail (excluding the favorite icon) opens the photo in the lightbox viewer. Clicking the favorite icon toggles the favorite status for that photo by calling addPhotoToFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites as appropriate and updating the UI based on the response.
data_binding: FavoritePhoto (via getGalleryPhotos.photos.is_favorited and favorite interface responses)

components.functionality: Shows pagination for the gallery based on the page, page_size, and total_results values returned from getGalleryPhotos. Calculates the available pages and indicates the currently active page. When the user selects a different page, it updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving current year and category, then navigates to gallery.html so that the page controller reloads photos by calling getGalleryPhotos with the new page value.

Provides an overlay or in-page large viewer for a single photo. It receives the currently selected Photo object and its is_favorited state from the gallery-photo-grid component without making additional API calls. It allows the user to navigate to the previous or next photo within the currently loaded photo set, and to toggle favorite status by calling addPhotoToFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites. Changes to favorite status from the lightbox are propagated back to the grid so both remain consistent.
data_binding: FavoritePhoto (via is_favorited state and favorite interface responses)

components.functionality: Displays a My favorites navigation element within the gallery content area, optionally showing the current count of favorited photos if total_favorite_photos is available from recent favorite operations. When clicked, it navigates to favorites.html without additional parameters. The component does not initiate any data loading itself but may update its displayed count when addPhotoToFavorites or removePhotoFromFavorites responses are received.
data_binding: FavoritePhoto (total count derived from addPhotoToFavorites.total_favorite_photos and removePhotoFromFavorites.total_favorite_photos)

components.functionality: Shows contextual links from the gallery to related articles in the Training or Competitions categories, based on the currently selected gallery category. When the active category matches one of the supported article categories, the component displays a link that navigates to articles.html with the categoryId query parameter set accordingly. This component relies solely on the active category state and does not call any interfaces.

Implements the header search box available on the gallery page. It collects a free-text search query (and optionally an article category filter if the header design includes such a field). On submit, it prevents default submission behavior and navigates to search_results.html with the query, page set to 1, and pageSize set by global configuration; an optional category parameter may be added based on the form inputs. This component does not call any search-related interfaces and does not alter gallery filters.
data_binding: URL (target search_results.html with query, category, page, pageSize)

components.functionality: Provides main site navigation links (such as Home, Photos, Favorites, Contact) while on the gallery page. Clicking these links navigates to index.html, gallery.html, favorites.html, or contact.html respectively. The Photos item corresponds to the current page and is visually indicated as active by the presentation layer but requires no additional data for functionality.
data_binding: URL (targets for index.html, gallery.html, favorites.html, contact.html)

components.functionality: Handles newsletter subscription from the gallery page footer. It presents fields for name, email, topic preferences (mapped to wants_training_tips, wants_competition_updates, wants_merchandise_offers), frequency, and email_format. On form submission, it calls createNewsletterSubscription with the current values. It interprets the success and message fields from the response to display confirmation or error messages and may clear or preserve form values based on the outcome.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a two-column layout with a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar and a wide, fluid right content canvas. The left column holds the decorative logo (handled outside these components), the vertical main navigation, and the prominent link to Favorites. The right column begins with a slim header band containing a subtle horizontal rule, the page title (implicit in the page controller), and the search field. Directly beneath this header band sits the filter bar, followed by the photo grid as the dominant body content, pagination at the bottom of the grid, then related article links and finally the newsletter form near the lower part of the right column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a two-column layout with a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar and a wide, fluid right content canvas. The left column holds the decorative logo (handled outside these components), the vertical main navigation, and the prominent link to Favorites. The right column begins with a slim header band containing a subtle horizontal rule, the page title (implicit in the page controller), and the search field. Directly beneath this header band sits the filter bar, followed by the photo grid as the dominant body content, pagination at the bottom of the grid, then related article links and finally the newsletter form near the lower part of the right column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The left navigation column occupies roughly 20% of the viewport width (with a min-width for usability and a max to keep it narrow), while the right content area takes the remaining ~80%. Vertically, the top-left is reserved for logo and main navigation items in the upper half of the sidebar; the Favorites link sits within this nav stack but slightly separated; the lower half of the sidebar remains mostly empty. In the right column, the top 10–15% of the viewport height is an airy header + filter zone, while the central 60–70% is dedicated to the photo grid. Pagination, related links, and the newsletter form share the remaining lower portion with generous vertical whitespace between them. The lightbox overlays the entire viewport when active.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density remains low and airy. Each navigation item in the sidebar has ample vertical padding and breathing room. The header search and filter bar are sparsely laid out with plenty of space around controls, avoiding clutter. The photo grid itself is denser than the rest of the page but still maintains generous gutters between thumbnails and margin around the grid, so it feels like a curated gallery rather than a packed photo wall. Pagination, related links, and newsletter form each have clear separation with 32–48px vertical gaps between sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the top-left with the logo and the vertical main navigation. The eye travels down the nav stack, noticing the dedicated Favorites link as a key destination. From there, the thin horizontal rule at the top of the right column gently pulls the eye across to the header band and the search control. The user then moves downward into the filter bar and the photo grid, which becomes the primary focal area. After scrolling through photos, the eye naturally moves to the centered pagination, then down to related articles, and finally to the newsletter form at the bottom. When a photo is opened, the lightbox temporarily dominates the visual flow, dimming all background content.

layout.overall_description: The page is structured as a calm, editorial two-column layout. On the left, a narrow vertical rail hosts the ornate green plaque logo at the very top (outside these components), followed by a simple, all-caps main navigation stack with equal-weight items and a clearly separated Favorites link. This sidebar occupies about a fifth of the screen, anchoring the experience visually while leaving much of the canvas open. To the right, a thin horizontal rule sits slightly below the logo’s vertical level, marking the top of a wide, mostly white content field. Within this right-hand canvas, a minimal header band contains the page-level context and a discrete search field aligned to the top-right. Below, an understated filter bar stretches partway across the width, and beneath it the photo grid takes over as the main attraction, filling the central area with evenly spaced thumbnails and comfortable gutters. Pagination controls sit centered under the grid with ample breathing room, and further down, a small block of related-article links subtly invites deeper reading. The bottom of the right column hosts a low-key newsletter form, aligned with the same generous margins and spacing. At any point, opening a photo launches a full-viewport lightbox, dimming the layout underneath while preserving the minimalist feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overarching two-column structure. It positions a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar on the far left (approximately 20% of the viewport width, with padding on the inside) and a wide, fluid right content area that fills the remaining 80%. The vertical alignment ensures that the top of the sidebar (logo + nav) lines up with the top of the right content header band. Within the right column, the controller establishes a vertical stack: header band at the top, then filter bar, then photo grid, followed by pagination controls, related-articles links, and finally the newsletter form as the last prominent block before the page’s bottom margin. The controller also reserves space above the main content for the thin horizontal rule that visually separates the header band from the rest of the blank canvas, ensuring large margins around all sections to keep the layout light and spacious.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static layout container that anchors the two-column structure. The left sidebar remains visually fixed relative to the viewport (using sticky or fixed positioning), while the right content column scrolls vertically within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main navigation sits in the left sidebar directly beneath the logo block, forming a vertical stack of simple, all-caps text links. It occupies the upper-left quadrant of the page and runs down the left column, using roughly the upper half of the sidebar height. Each nav item is a single line, left-aligned, with ample vertical spacing (roughly one line-height plus 8–12px padding above and below) so each label is clearly separated. One item (e.g., the Photos section) includes a subtle down-caret to indicate expandable subnavigation, matching the ‘OUR RIDES’ pattern from the mockup. The nav column itself has a 16–24px inner left/right padding from the page edge, aligning it with the logo and other sidebar elements. Below this nav stack, a comfortable vertical gap leads down to the Favorites link, keeping the main nav visually cohesive and distinct from secondary sidebar actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sidebar nav is sticky within the left column, remaining visible near the top of the viewport as the user scrolls the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header search is placed in the right column’s slim header band, aligned to the top-right edge of the content area. It sits on the same horizontal level as the thin rule that separates the header band from the rest of the page, with the rule running behind or just above it. The search field occupies about 25–30% of the right column’s width on desktop and is positioned with generous whitespace: a 32–40px margin from the top of the page and about 32px from the right edge. It does not span the full width; instead, it feels like a light control floating in the open white space, letting the rest of the header area remain mostly empty. There is a comfortable gap (24–32px) beneath the search area before the filter bar begins, reinforcing the low-density, editorial ambiance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column’s header band. It scrolls away with the rest of the content, optionally becoming sticky only within the first few hundred pixels if desired, but visually it behaves like a simple, non-dominant header control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits directly below the header band in the right content column, aligned to the left edge of the photo grid. It begins roughly 24–32px beneath the horizontal rule/header area, giving it breathing space. Horizontally, the filter bar spans about 60–70% of the right column’s width, starting from the left margin of the content area and leaving some open white space on the far right to preserve the airy feel. Inside the bar, year and category filters are laid out in a single horizontal row, left-aligned, with 16–24px gaps between filter controls. The visual treatment is minimal: slim text or pill-style selectors sitting on the blank background rather than a heavy, boxed strip. There is another 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the filter bar and the top row of the photo grid, so the grid feels clearly separated yet closely related to the filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a semi-sticky toolbar within the right column: it remains in the normal document flow but can become sticky at the top of the viewport (just below any persistent header) as the user scrolls, anchoring filters close to the gallery content without crowding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The photo grid is the dominant content block in the right column, occupying most of the central vertical space. It is positioned directly below the filter bar with a generous 24–32px gap between them. The grid spans most of the right column’s width, leaving 32–48px side margins from the content edges to mirror the airy editorial layout. Thumbnails are arranged in a responsive grid: on larger screens, roughly 3–4 columns with equal-width cells and consistent 16–24px gutters between both rows and columns. The grid extends downward, with rows of photos creating a balanced mosaic that still preserves white space around each image. The top of the grid visually aligns with the left margin of the filter bar, creating a clean vertical flow. Vertical spacing between the last row of thumbnails and the pagination controls is again generous (32–40px), ensuring the grid feels like its own strong block of content rather than merging into navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block content that scrolls within the right column. It expands vertically as more photos are loaded, maintaining consistent column widths and gutters while adapting to different viewport widths.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit directly below the photo grid in the right column, centered horizontally in the available width. They are separated from the bottom of the grid by about 32–40px of whitespace, so they clearly appear as navigational controls rather than part of the last row of photos. The pagination element itself is relatively compact, occupying at most 30–40% of the right column’s width, with page numbers or next/previous buttons arranged in a single horizontal line. Above and below the controls there is ample vertical space, helping them feel unobtrusive and lightweight in the layout. The pagination aligns visually with the center of the photo grid area, maintaining the calm, balanced aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned at the end of the current page of grid content, moving downward as more content or additional blocks (like related links) are added. It scrolls with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related articles links appear below the pagination controls in the right column, acting as a soft transition from the gallery into more narrative content. They are positioned with a generous vertical gap (40–48px) beneath the pagination, ensuring clear separation. The component is left-aligned with the main content column, starting at the same left margin as the photo grid and filter bar. It occupies roughly the left 50–60% of the right column’s width, forming a short vertical list of 3–5 textual links, each on its own line with 12–16px vertical spacing. The right side of this area remains mostly empty, echoing the mockup’s open canvas. This treatment keeps related links visually lightweight, more like editorial suggestions than a heavy sidebar module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block that sits toward the lower portion of the right column, scrolling with the page and leaving plenty of blank space around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter form is the last major element in the right content column, placed below the related articles links. It begins after another 40–48px vertical gap, making it feel like a distinct footer-like section of the page. Horizontally, the form spans about 60–70% of the right column’s width and is centered or slightly left-biased to keep alignment with the main content column. The input field(s) and submit button are arranged in a simple linear layout (email field plus a subtle button), with plenty of padding inside the form area (16–24px) but no heavy box or background, allowing the form to sit lightly on the white canvas. There is ample white space above and below the form, and a comfortable bottom margin before the actual page footer, reinforcing the calm, unhurried feel of the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-like block anchored at the lower end of the right column’s content. It scrolls normally and provides a clear endpoint to the page’s main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Favorites link is integrated into the left sidebar beneath the main navigation stack but given a slight visual separation to indicate its special function. It appears as a single, all-caps text link aligned with the other nav items, occupying a full-width line in the left column. There is a larger vertical gap (24–32px) between the last main nav item and the Favorites link, and a smaller gap (16–24px) below it before the beginning of the blank lower part of the sidebar. The link is positioned around the middle of the sidebar’s height, still well within the user’s initial viewport. This placement makes it easy to jump to the Favorites page without crowding the primary nav items, and it stands out because of the spacing rather than any heavy styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar navigation stack, moving with the nav when it sticks to the top of the viewport. It behaves like a standard navigation element but with more breathing room around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The photo lightbox is a full-viewport overlay that sits above both the left sidebar and the right content column when activated. It covers the entire screen with a semi-transparent dark backdrop, dimming the underlying minimalist layout. At the center of the viewport, a large image area displays the selected photo, sized to fit within about 70–80% of the viewport width and height while maintaining its aspect ratio. The image is surrounded by generous negative space, with controls (close icon, navigation arrows, and a favorite toggle) placed lightly around the edges of the image—top-right for close, left/right for arrows, and a small favorite icon at a corner—so they don’t overpower the photo. The underlying two-column layout remains visible but subdued beneath the overlay, emphasizing that the lightbox is a temporary, focused viewing mode.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay anchored to the viewport, centered horizontally and vertically. It stays in place as the user scrolls (if scrolling within the overlay is allowed) and completely intercepts interaction with the underlying page until closed.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for sending questions or feedback to the blog owner, with inquiry type selection, optional article association, and newsletter subscription in the footer.

core_features: Display a contact form with fields for name, email, subject, message, inquiry type selector, and an optional field to capture the related article’s published date text.
Fetch available inquiry type options from getContactFormOptions and bind them dynamically to the inquiry type dropdown.
Use the incoming URL parameters relatedArticleId and subject to pre-associate the message with an article and prefill the subject field.

Submit contact messages via submitContactMessage, passing all required fields plus optional relatedArticleId and related_article_published_date_text, and show inline confirmation or error feedback on the same page.
Provide a footer newsletter subscription form that uses createNewsletterSubscription to create a newsletter subscription with selected topics, frequency, and email format.
Expose global navigation and header search so users can move from the contact page to other parts of the site using URL-based navigation parameters.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Ask a general or nutrition-related question without article context: Step 1 - User navigates to contact.html via header navigation or a direct link without URL parameters; Step 2 - Page initializes the contact form and calls getContactFormOptions to populate the inquiry type dropdown; Step 3 - User fills in name, email, subject, message, optionally the related article published date text, and chooses an inquiry type; Step 4 - User submits the form; Step 5 - Page calls submitContactMessage with the entered data and no relatedArticleId (because none was provided in the URL); Step 6 - On success, the page displays a confirmation state using the response from submitContactMessage while remaining on contact.html; Step 7 - User can optionally click header navigation to go to another page.

Workflow 2 – Ask a question about a specific article using a link from that article: Step 1 - User clicks a 'Contact about this article' link on article_detail.html which navigates to contact.html with relatedArticleId in the URL and may include a subject parameter; Step 2 - Contact page reads relatedArticleId and subject from the URL; Step 3 - Contact form initializes with subject field prefilled from the subject parameter and stores relatedArticleId in hidden state for later submission; Step 4 - Page calls getContactFormOptions and renders the inquiry type dropdown; Step 5 - User fills in remaining fields (name, email, message, optionally the related article published date text and/or adjusts subject and inquiry type); Step 6 - User submits the form; Step 7 - Page calls submitContactMessage with all fields, including relatedArticleId and any related_article_published_date_text the user provided; Step 8 - On success, the confirmation state is shown inline and the user can stay on the page or navigate elsewhere using header links.

Workflow 3 – Use the header search from the contact page: Step 1 - User enters a query (and optionally selects a category filter) into the header search form on contact.html; Step 2 - User submits the search form; Step 3 - Browser navigates to search_results.html with query and optional category parameters along with page and pageSize initialized via URL; Step 4 - User views search results, leaving the contact page.

Workflow 4 – Navigate to content categories or home from the contact page: Step 1 - User clicks a navigation item in the header such as Home, a specific articles category, Photos, Reading list, or Favorites; Step 2 - Browser navigates to the corresponding target page (index.html, articles.html with categoryId, gallery.html, reading_list.html, favorites.html) using URL parameters defined for that target; Step 3 - The contact page is left and no additional API calls are made from contact.html after navigation.

Workflow 5 – Subscribe to the newsletter via the contact page footer: Step 1 - User scrolls to the footer on contact.html and locates the newsletter subscription form; Step 2 - User enters name and email, selects topic checkboxes, chooses a frequency option, and chooses an email format option; Step 3 - User submits the newsletter form; Step 4 - Page calls createNewsletterSubscription with the entered data; Step 5 - On success, the page updates the footer area to show a success state using the API response while keeping the user on contact.html; Step 6 - On error, an error state is shown, allowing the user to correct inputs and resubmit.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters (relatedArticleId and subject) and initialize form state, including a stored relatedArticleId value and a prefilled subject field if present.
On page load or component initialization, call getContactFormOptions once and bind the returned inquiry_types list to the inquiry type dropdown options.

On any input change in the contact form (name, email, subject, message, inquiry type, related article date text), update the local form state so that submitContactMessage can be called with the current values.

On contact form submission, perform client-side validation for required fields; if valid, disable the submit button, set a 'submitting' state, and invoke submitContactMessage with all relevant parameters; re-enable the button after the API responds.

On successful submitContactMessage response, transition to a 'success' state on the same page, show the success message from the API, and optionally clear or lock the form fields while remaining on contact.html.
On failed submitContactMessage response, transition to an 'error' state, show the error message from the API, and keep the form values so the user can adjust and resubmit.
On newsletter form field changes (name, email, topic checkboxes, frequency, email format), update newsletter form state bound to the NewsletterSubscription model structure.

On newsletter form submission, validate required fields, call createNewsletterSubscription with the current state, and update the footer with success or error status based on the API response while keeping the user on contact.html.

On clicking header navigation links, navigate to the configured target pages (index.html, articles.html with categoryId, gallery.html, reading_list.html, favorites.html, contact.html) by updating the browser URL with appropriate query parameters.
On submitting the header search form, navigate to search_results.html and pass the query text and optional category as URL parameters, along with initial page and pageSize values.
page_functionality.state_logic: The contact page reads URL parameters on load: if a relatedArticleId parameter is present, it is stored in an internal state variable and later sent through submitContactMessage as the relatedArticleId parameter; this also controls whether an internal 'hasRelatedArticle' flag is true for showing article-related context messaging (without loading article data via any additional interface). If a subject parameter is present, the subject input is initialized with this value but remains editable. The inquiry type dropdown options are not hardcoded; instead, the page calls getContactFormOptions after initialization and binds the returned inquiry_types list to the dropdown. The currently selected inquiry_type is stored in form state and is submitted as-is to submitContactMessage. The contact form maintains a simple finite state: 'idle' (initial, fields editable, no ongoing request), 'submitting' (submit button disabled, blocking duplicate submissions), 'success' (API call returned success, show inline confirmation using the API's message field, optionally lock or clear inputs), and 'error' (API returned failure or network error, show inline error using the API's message field, keep inputs for editing). The related_article_published_date_text field is optional and is sent only if the user filled it out; it is not derived automatically because contact.html does not call any article-related interface. The newsletter subscription form in the footer has its own independent state, including fields aligned with the NewsletterSubscription parameters and status flags like 'idle', 'submitting', 'success', and 'error'; it does not share state with the contact form. Header navigation and search do not use any APIs on this page; they only modify browser location. Navigating away from contact.html via header links or search results clears any in-memory state naturally as the page unloads; returning to the contact page rebuilds state purely from the current URL parameters and fresh calls to getContactFormOptions.

components.functionality: Provides main site navigation from the contact page to other core pages. It includes links to the homepage, article categories, gallery, reading list, favorites, and the current contact page. Clicking these links updates the browser URL to the appropriate target paths with any required parameters (e.g., categoryId for articles.html) without calling page-specific interfaces.

Allows the user to perform a site-wide search directly from the contact page. When the user submits the search form, it navigates to search_results.html, passing the entered query, an optional category filter, and initializing page and pageSize via URL parameters.

Manages the dynamic inquiry type dropdown within the contact form. On initialization, it calls getContactFormOptions to retrieve the available inquiry_types and binds each returned value/label pair to dropdown options. The selected inquiry_type is stored in the contact form state and will be sent to submitContactMessage on form submission.

Reads the relatedArticleId URL parameter on page load and stores it in the contact form state for later submission. When relatedArticleId is present, this component marks the form state as being associated with an article so that submitContactMessage receives the relatedArticleId parameter. It can also conditionally render contextual text indicating that the inquiry will be linked to a specific article, without loading any article details.

Central component that collects the user’s contact details and inquiry information. It includes inputs for name, email, subject, message body, inquiry type (via the inquiry-type-selector component), and an optional text field where the user can enter the related article’s published date exactly as they saw it. On initialization, it pre-fills the subject field from the subject URL parameter if present. On submission, after local validation, it calls submitContactMessage with name, email, subject, message, inquiry_type, the stored relatedArticleId (if any), and related_article_published_date_text (if provided). It manages the form lifecycle states (idle, submitting, success, error).

Displays real-time status messages for the contact form, based on the current form state and the responses from submitContactMessage. In the 'submitting' state it can indicate that the form is processing. In the 'success' state it shows the success message returned by submitContactMessage and may visually confirm that the message was sent. In the 'error' state it shows the error message returned by submitContactMessage and prompts the user to review their input or try again.

Footer component that collects newsletter subscription details. It captures name, email, topic preferences mapped to wants_training_tips, wants_competition_updates, and wants_merchandise_offers boolean fields, as well as frequency and email_format selections. On submit, it calls createNewsletterSubscription with these values, and updates its own status state based on the API response, showing success or error feedback without leaving the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a two-region layout: a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar that holds the logo (outside the scope of these components) and the header-navigation stacked vertically, and a wide, open main content area on the right that contains the contact-related components. Within the right area, maintain a simple vertical flow: a slim header band with the header-search-form aligned to the top-right, a thin horizontal rule spanning the full width below that band, then the contact-form and its related elements (contact-form-status, inquiry-type-selector, related-article-context) stacked top-to-bottom with generous whitespace, followed by the newsletter-subscription-form in a light footer-like section at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a two-region layout: a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar that holds the logo (outside the scope of these components) and the header-navigation stacked vertically, and a wide, open main content area on the right that contains the contact-related components. Within the right area, maintain a simple vertical flow: a slim header band with the header-search-form aligned to the top-right, a thin horizontal rule spanning the full width below that band, then the contact-form and its related elements (contact-form-status, inquiry-type-selector, related-article-context) stacked top-to-bottom with generous whitespace, followed by the newsletter-subscription-form in a light footer-like section at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly 20% of the viewport width to the left sidebar and 80% to the right content area. The sidebar runs full height; the navigation occupies the upper half of this column, leaving blank space below. In the right area, the main interactive column (contact-form and related elements) uses about 55–60% of the right content width, left-aligned, leaving a large margin of empty space on the right to preserve the airy editorial feel. Vertical spacing between major sections (nav vs. main, header band vs. form, form vs. newsletter) follows a 32–48px rhythm based on the 8px scale; internal padding within panels (like the related-article-context) uses 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain very low density across the page. Elements are not crammed; each block has clear breathing room. Navigation items have ample vertical spacing (roughly one line height plus 8–12px), and form fields are stacked with 16–24px vertical gaps. The right content column is intentionally narrower than the available space, leaving broad blank margins, especially to the right of the form. Panels and banners avoid heavy borders and large blocks of color, instead using light lines and subtle accents to keep the layout feeling calm and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the left sidebar at the top (logo area, then header-navigation), flowing downward through the stacked nav items. From there, the thin horizontal rule and the subtle presence of the header-search-form at the top-right gently pull attention into the right content area. Within the main column, the contact-form-status (when present) sits immediately above the contact-form, becoming the first in-flow element users see, followed by the inquiry-type-selector near the top of the form, then the related-article-context, and finally the rest of the form fields. After the form, the eye naturally continues down to the newsletter-subscription-form in the lower portion of the page, completing a predominantly vertical reading path with a single early horizontal diversion toward the search in the header band.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a quiet, editorial layout: a slim vertical rail on the left holds the navigation, while a large, mostly white canvas stretches to the right. At the top-left, the logo (outside the scope of these components) anchors the page; directly beneath it, the header-navigation forms a tidy vertical list of all-caps links with even spacing, extending through the upper half of the sidebar and leaving intentionally empty space below. To the right, aligned with the top of the viewport, a slender header band runs across the page; the header-search-form sits in the top-right corner of this band, small and unobtrusive. Just below, a thin horizontal rule extends from the inner edge of the sidebar across the full width of the content area, marking the start of the main content. Under this rule, slightly inset from the left edge of the right area, a relatively narrow contact column begins: at its top, the contact-form-status (when visible) appears as a subtle banner, followed by the contact-form itself. The inquiry-type-selector is placed near the top of the form, with the related-article-context panel directly beneath it as a light, framed block that does not visually dominate. The remainder of the form fields continue downward in a single, clean column with generous spacing. Below this main contact block, separated by a large vertical gap, the newsletter-subscription-form appears as a low-profile, horizontally oriented card aligned to the same left margin as the contact form, leaving wide white space to the right and bottom. Overall, the design keeps the user’s focus on the content in the upper-left and upper-middle of the screen, while the expansive empty areas around these elements preserve the minimalist, contemplative feel of a personal blog.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation sits in the fixed left sidebar column, directly beneath the logo area at the top-left of the page. The sidebar occupies roughly 20% of the viewport width and runs full height. Within this column, the navigation forms a single vertical stack of links, starting about 32px below the bottom edge of the logo. Each item is left-aligned, set in a single line of all-caps text, and spans nearly the full width of the sidebar with 8–16px horizontal padding from the sidebar edge. Vertically, each navigation item has about 8–12px of padding above and below, plus a small 8–16px gap between items, creating a clear, evenly spaced list that fills roughly the upper half of the sidebar. A small downward caret icon appears to the right of the ‘OUR RIDES’ label, aligned to the same baseline, but kept visually subtle. The lower half of the sidebar remains mostly empty, with only the background visible, reinforcing the sparse editorial character.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The left sidebar, including the header-navigation, is fixed in place relative to the viewport on desktop (using a fixed or sticky position), so it remains visible as the user scrolls the main content on the right. The navigation items themselves do not scroll independently; they move only if the entire page scrolls on smaller viewports where the sidebar behavior may adapt.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form is positioned in the top-right corner of the main content area, inside a slim header band that spans the full width of the right column. The right content area begins immediately to the right of the sidebar, occupying about 80% of the viewport width. The search form is aligned flush to the right edge of this area with approximately 24–32px of padding from the top edge of the viewport and 24px from the right edge. It appears as a compact, unobtrusive search field, occupying roughly 35–40% of the width of the right content area, leaving plenty of blank space to its left. About 16–24px below the baseline of the search input, a thin, light horizontal rule runs from the inner edge of the sidebar column all the way to the right edge of the content area, creating a clean visual divider between the header band and the main contact content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header-search-form stays anchored near the top of the right content area; as the page scrolls, it moves out of view with the rest of the header band. It does not remain sticky, maintaining the focus on the contact form once the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inquiry-type-selector is placed inside the main contact-form block, near the top of the form fields. The contact-form itself is a vertically oriented panel that starts around 48–64px below the horizontal rule in the right content area. The form’s content column is left-aligned within the right area and spans about 55–60% of the right content width, leaving a broad margin of white space on the right. At the top of this column, after the form’s title and short introductory text (if present), the inquiry-type-selector appears as the first or second field, spanning the full width of the form column. It is treated as a standard select field with a label above it; the label has about 8px of space above the selector field itself, and there is 16–24px of vertical spacing below before the next element (the related-article-context panel). The selector follows the same minimalist styling as other inputs: thin border, generous internal padding, and plenty of breathing room around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The inquiry-type-selector scrolls naturally with the contact form content. It remains visually anchored near the top of the form and does not exhibit any sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-article-context component appears directly below the inquiry-type-selector within the contact-form column, maintaining the single-column, vertical flow. It is presented as a light, unobtrusive panel that occupies the same width as the other form fields (about 55–60% of the right content width) and is left-aligned with them. There is about 16–24px of vertical gap between the bottom of the inquiry-type selector and the top of this panel. Inside the panel, content is framed with 16–24px of internal padding and possibly a subtle, thin border or a slightly tinted background to distinguish it from the pure white page while keeping the overall look minimal. The panel’s height is relatively compact—roughly the height of 2–3 lines of text plus internal padding—so it feels like a contextual note rather than a large card. Below the panel, another 24px gap separates it from the next form field (such as subject or message), ensuring the context does not crowd the main form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The related-article-context panel is fully embedded in the form’s vertical flow and scrolls with the contact form. It does not float or stick; when no article is associated, the component either collapses entirely (removing its vertical space) or appears as a very small placeholder row, keeping the surrounding whitespace consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form serves as the primary content block in the right-hand area. It is positioned below the header band and horizontal rule, starting approximately 48–64px beneath the rule to maintain a generous top margin. The form is left-aligned within the right content area, with its left edge roughly aligned to the beginning of the horizontal rule (just to the right of the sidebar boundary). The form’s width is constrained to about 55–60% of the right content area, leaving a broad, empty margin on the right side that reinforces the minimalist aesthetic. Within this column, the form fields stack vertically: title and brief intro (if present), then the contact-form-status (when applicable) just above the fields, followed by the inquiry-type-selector, the related-article-context panel, and the remaining fields (e.g., name, email, message). Each field has 16–24px vertical spacing from the next, with labels set immediately above inputs and 8px of space between label and field. The bottom of the form, including the submit button, sits well above the fold on taller screens but has at least 48px of whitespace beneath it before any subsequent section to prevent visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The contact-form scrolls normally within the right content area. It is not fixed or sticky; as the user scrolls down, the entire form moves in unison, maintaining its relative position above the newsletter-subscription-form and below the header band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-status component appears as a horizontal banner embedded directly above the main stack of contact-form fields, within the same column width as the form (about 55–60% of the right content area). When visible, it sits approximately 16–24px below the form title and intro text and about 16–24px above the inquiry-type-selector. The banner spans the full width of the form column but has a modest height—just enough for a single line or two of text plus 12–16px of vertical padding. Its styling is subtle, relying on a light background tint and a thin border or accent line rather than heavy color blocks, so it communicates status without overpowering the minimalist layout. The edges align perfectly with the other form fields, preserving a clean vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The contact-form-status appears and disappears inline, pushing form fields up or down but not overlaying them. It scrolls with the rest of the form, and when not active, the space collapses entirely so the form fields slide up to fill the gap, keeping the vertical flow continuous.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-subscription-form is positioned in the lower part of the right content area, functioning like a light footer section. It appears below the contact-form, with a generous vertical gap of about 64px separating the bottom of the contact-form (including its submit button and any small notes) from the top of the newsletter block. The newsletter form is left-aligned with the contact-form, sharing the same left margin and occupying a similar or slightly wider width—about 60–65% of the right content area—to give it a subtle, more horizontal feel. Internally, it uses a compact arrangement: a short headline or label on the left and the email input plus subscribe button arranged either on a single line or in a tight vertical stack, while still preserving 16–24px of padding inside the component. Ample white space remains to the right and below, so this section feels gentle and optional rather than dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The newsletter-subscription-form is part of the normal document flow at the bottom of the main content column and scrolls into view as the user moves past the contact-form. It does not stick to the viewport edges or float; instead, it acts as a calm, concluding block on the page.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Page that displays article search results based on the URL query parameters, lets users refine the search, paginate through results, and manage reading list and favorite articles, plus subscribe to the newsletter from the footer.

core_features: Read the search parameters (query, category, page, pageSize) from the URL and execute the searchArticles interface on initial load and whenever these parameters change.

Display a list of search results with article titles, categories, tags, excerpts/summaries, published dates, and optional metadata such as reading time and thumbnails, using data returned from searchArticles.
Allow users to refine the search query and category via a search form on this page, then update the URL parameters and reload results using searchArticles.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter so users can move between pages of search results without losing their search term or category filter.
Enable users to open any article from the results by clicking its title, navigating to article_detail.html with the correct articleId parameter.

Allow users to add or remove articles from their reading list directly from each result item using saveArticleToReadingList and removeArticleFromReadingList, reflecting the current state from searchArticles.
Allow users to add or remove articles from their favorites directly from each result item using addArticleToFavorites and removeArticleFromFavorites, reflecting the current state from searchArticles.
Provide a newsletter subscription form in the footer that creates a newsletter subscription via createNewsletterSubscription with configurable topics, frequency, and email format.

Offer navigation to other main sections (Home, Articles, Photos, Reading list, Favorites, Contact, About) via header and footer links, preserving or resetting search context as defined by the navigation rules.
user_workflows: Search from another page and view results: 1) User enters a term in a site-wide search box on any page and submits. 2) Browser navigates to search_results.html with query (and optionally category) in the URL. 3) On load, Search Results page reads query, category, page, and pageSize from the URL. 4) Page calls searchArticles with these parameters. 5) Page displays the returned results list and summary (total results, current page).

Refine search directly on Search Results: 1) User adjusts the query text input and/or category selector in the on-page search form. 2) User submits the form. 3) Page builds a new URL for search_results.html with updated query and category, page set to the first page, and pageSize preserved or adjusted based on the form. 4) Browser navigates to the new URL. 5) On load, the page calls searchArticles with the new parameters and shows the updated results.

Paginate through results: 1) User is viewing a page of results with pagination controls rendered from searchArticles metadata (total_results, page, page_size). 2) User clicks a pagination control (next/previous or a specific page number). 3) Page updates the URL page parameter (keeping query, category, and pageSize the same) and navigates to search_results.html with the new page. 4) On load, searchArticles is called with the updated page number, and the new result slice is displayed.

Open an article from results: 1) While viewing search results, user clicks an article title or dedicated 'Open' action in a result item. 2) Page navigates to article_detail.html with articleId set to the id of the clicked article (using the Article.id from the searchArticles result). 3) Article Detail page loads and displays the full article using its own logic.

Manage reading list from Search Results: 1) User views a result item that shows its current reading list status based on is_in_reading_list from searchArticles. 2) If not in reading list, user clicks the 'Save to reading list' control. 3) Component calls saveArticleToReadingList with the articleId. 4) On success, the component updates its local state for that result item to reflect that it is now in the reading list. 5) If the article is already in the list, user can click the corresponding 'Remove' control instead. 6) Component calls removeArticleFromReadingList with articleId and, on success, updates the reading list state for that item.

Manage favorites from Search Results: 1) User views a result item that shows its current favorite status based on is_favorited from searchArticles. 2) If not favorited, user clicks the 'Add to favorites' control. 3) Component calls addArticleToFavorites with articleId. 4) On success, the component updates its local favorite state for that result item. 5) If already favorited, user clicks the 'Remove from favorites' control. 6) Component calls removeArticleFromFavorites with articleId and, on success, updates the state to show it is no longer a favorite.

Subscribe to newsletter from the Search Results page: 1) User scrolls to the footer newsletter subscription form. 2) User fills in name and email and selects topics (training tips, competition updates, merchandise offers), a frequency, and an email format. 3) User submits the form. 4) Component calls createNewsletterSubscription with the provided values. 5) On success, component can show a dynamic confirmation state indicating subscription was created, without hardcoded messaging content.

Navigate to category-based article lists from the sidebar: 1) User opens the search results page and sees category links in the sidebar or header. 2) User clicks a category (for example, a training or health category link). 3) Browser navigates to articles.html with categoryId set to the chosen category value. 4) Articles page loads and uses its own logic to filter articles by category.

Access the Search Results page from the footer 'Search' link: 1) User clicks the 'Search' link in the footer. 2) Page navigates to search_results.html, with query optionally empty or prefilled as configured externally. 3) If a query is present, searchArticles is called; if not, the page may render an empty state or prompt the user to search using the on-page search form.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters query, category, page, and pageSize from the current location and invoke searchArticles with those values to populate the initial results state.

When the search form on the page is submitted (onSubmit), prevent default browser form behavior, construct a URL to search_results.html with updated query and category parameters, set page to the first page, preserve or update pageSize based on user input, and then navigate to that URL.

When a pagination control is clicked (onClick), determine the target page number from the control, construct a URL to search_results.html with the same query, category, and pageSize, but with page set to the target page, and navigate to that URL, triggering a new searchArticles call on load.
When a user clicks a search result title (onClick), navigate to article_detail.html with articleId set to the Article.id from the associated searchArticles result object.

When the user toggles the reading list control on a result item (onClick), choose between saveArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList based on the current is_in_reading_list state for that result; call the appropriate interface with articleId, then update the item’s local reading list state using the success response.

When the user toggles the favorites control on a result item (onClick), choose between addArticleToFavorites or removeArticleFromFavorites based on the current is_favorited state for that result; call the appropriate interface with articleId, then update the item’s local favorite state using the success response.

When the user changes the category filter via a dropdown in the search form (onChange), store the selected category value in component state; this value will be included in the next search form submission to update the category URL parameter and re-run searchArticles.

When the user clicks a category link in the sidebar (onClick) that leads to broader category exploration, navigate directly to articles.html with categoryId set to the clicked category value without calling any Search Results interfaces.

When the user submits the newsletter subscription form in the footer (onSubmit), collect the name, email, and topic/frequency/format preferences from the form, call createNewsletterSubscription with these values, and on completion update the component state to show success or error feedback based on the returned success field.

When the user uses header navigation links (Home, Photos, Reading list, Favorites, Contact), navigate to the respective target pages (index.html, gallery.html, reading_list.html, favorites.html, contact.html) without modifying or using the Search Results interfaces; any subsequent searches from those pages will again navigate back with appropriate URL parameters.
When the user clicks the About link in the footer (onClick), navigate to about.html, leaving the Search Results context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses, without localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. The URL parameters query (string), category (string), page (number), and pageSize (number) form the canonical state for the search. On initial render and on every navigation to search_results.html, the page controller reads these parameters from the URL. If query is present, it is passed directly into searchArticles, and the search is executed; if query is absent or empty, the page may choose not to call searchArticles until the user submits the on-page search form, or it may call searchArticles with an empty query depending on global configuration (not hardcoded here). If category is provided, it is passed into searchArticles to filter results by the corresponding Article.category; if category is missing, searchArticles is called without a category filter so that all categories matching the query may be returned. The page parameter controls which slice of results should be shown. If page is missing or invalid, the page logic treats it as the first page for the purpose of building the searchArticles call and pagination controls; after navigation due to user interaction, page is always explicitly set in the URL. The pageSize parameter controls how many results are requested per page; if it is absent, the logic uses a system-level default page size defined elsewhere in the application, not hardcoded in this page specification. Whenever the user submits the on-page search form, the page constructs a new URL that encodes the updated query and category from the form, resets page to the first page, and uses either the existing pageSize value or the user-selected pageSize if the UI allows it. Whenever the user interacts with pagination, the page constructs a new URL where query, category, and pageSize remain identical to the current URL, and only page is updated to the target page number. Navigation to other pages (Article Detail, Articles list, Gallery, Reading list, Favorites, Contact, About, or Home) does not preserve the Search Results state unless those target pages explicitly include search links back to search_results.html with fresh parameters; Search Results state is re-established each time from the URL plus fresh searchArticles responses. Reading list and favorite status for each result item are not persisted in URL parameters; instead, the initial state is taken from is_in_reading_list and is_favorited in the searchArticles response, and subsequent toggling of these states relies on calling the appropriate interfaces and updating local component state for the current page view without changing the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial loading of search data by reading URL parameters and calling searchArticles, then distributes the response to child components for rendering the search summary, results list, and pagination.

Provides a persistent search form at the top of the Search Results page, prefilled from the current query and category URL parameters, allowing users to refine or start a new search and then navigate to search_results.html with updated parameters.

Displays a textual summary of the current search, such as the query term, selected category, total_results, and the current page relative to the total number of pages, using metadata from the searchArticles response.

Renders the list of search results returned in searchArticles.results, delegating each result item to a dedicated result item component and passing down article data, tags, and state flags (is_in_reading_list, is_favorited).

Displays an individual article result including its title, category_name, tags, summary/excerpt, optional thumbnail image, reading_time_minutes, and published date. Provides controls to open the article detail page, toggle inclusion in the reading list, and toggle favorites, using state from searchArticles and updates from the respective interfaces.

Uses total_results, page, and page_size from the searchArticles response to compute pagination and render controls for moving to previous/next pages or specific page numbers. Clicking a control updates the page URL parameter and reloads the page, which triggers a new searchArticles request.

Provides links or selection options for article categories. For search-scoped filtering, it synchronizes with the category parameter and the search-bar-header component; for broader category exploration, it includes links that navigate to articles.html with the appropriate categoryId.

Offers main navigation links (Home, Articles, Photos, Reading list, Favorites, Contact) that allow users to leave the Search Results page and access other parts of the site without invoking any of this page’s interfaces.
Displays footer navigation including links to Search and About, enabling navigation to search_results.html (with empty or prefilled query) and about.html.

Collects newsletter subscription details (name, email, topics, frequency, and email format) and calls createNewsletterSubscription. On success or failure, it updates its own state to show feedback using the returned success and message fields, without hardcoded content.

Abstracts the logic of adding/removing articles from the reading list for use by search-result-item. Given an articleId and current is_in_reading_list state, it calls either saveArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList and returns updated state to the item component.

Abstracts the logic of adding/removing articles from favorites for use by search-result-item. Given an articleId and current is_favorited state, it calls either addArticleToFavorites or removeArticleFromFavorites and returns updated state to the item component.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the original two-rail structure: a narrow, fixed/sticky left column for the logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, mostly open content canvas on the right. The right side hosts a simple, top-to-bottom stack: a thin horizontal rule at the top of the content panel, then the search bar, search summary, and below that a main search area where a slim filter sidebar hugs the left of the content column and the results list occupies the rest of the space to its right. Pagination sits at the bottom of the results, and a low-key full-width footer spans below everything.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the original two-rail structure: a narrow, fixed/sticky left column for the logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, mostly open content canvas on the right. The right side hosts a simple, top-to-bottom stack: a thin horizontal rule at the top of the content panel, then the search bar, search summary, and below that a main search area where a slim filter sidebar hugs the left of the content column and the results list occupies the rest of the space to its right. Pagination sits at the bottom of the results, and a low-key full-width footer spans below everything.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Left navigation column takes roughly 18–22% of the viewport width (with a practical min/max such as 240–320px) and runs full height. The right content area takes the remaining 78–82%, centered in the remaining horizontal space. Within the right area, the horizontal rule spans the full content width. The search bar and summary sit in the upper-left portion, occupying about 60–70% of the right column width. Below, the category filter sidebar uses roughly 22–26% of the right column width, while the results list uses the remaining 74–78%. Vertical spacing follows an 8px base: 32–48px gaps between major sections; 16–24px internal padding for cards and key controls.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain very low density: few simultaneous elements, generous white space, and no visual crowding. Text-based controls are spaced with at least 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px between groups. Results items are separated by 24–32px, with plenty of breathing room around the filter sidebar and pagination. The left nav occupies only the upper part of its column, leaving noticeable empty space below to keep the calm, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the green plaque logo in the upper-left corner, then travels downward through the stacked navigation links. From there, the thin horizontal rule along the top of the right column leads the eye to the search bar around the upper-left of the main area. The user then reads downward through the summary, into the filter sidebar and the vertical stack of results items. Secondary attention is drawn to the small action controls (favorites and reading list) on each result, and finally to the pagination at the bottom. The footer is visually quiet, discovered when the user scrolls to the page end.

layout.overall_description: The page is split into two clear vertical rails: on the far left, a narrow, off-white column holds the ornate green plaque logo at the very top and a short stack of uppercase navigation links beneath it. This column is mostly empty below the nav, emphasizing the quiet, minimal character of the site. To its right, a wide white canvas serves as the search results area, introduced by a thin, light horizontal rule running from the left edge of the content column across to the right, slightly below the logo’s vertical position. Just below this rule in the upper-left of the main content, the search bar sits as a simple, wide input with lots of space around it, followed by a concise summary line. Under that, the content splits lightly: a slim, text-only filter sidebar in the left portion of the content column and, to its right, a tall vertical stack of airy result cards, each with clear titles, short excerpts, and small favorite/reading-list icons tucked to the right. The results scroll down the page with generous gaps, culminating in a centered pagination row. At the very bottom, stretching across under both columns, a low, unobtrusive footer offers a small block of about/links on the left and a quiet newsletter subscription form on the right, all with ample padding and plenty of negative space around them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the full-page layout shell. It establishes a horizontal two-column flex layout: a fixed-width left rail for the header navigation, and a flexible right column for all search-related content. The left rail is anchored to the left edge of the viewport and runs from the top to the bottom, while the right content area begins immediately to the right of this column and stretches to the right edge of the viewport. Vertical scrolling occurs within the right content area and the overall page, while the left rail remains visually constant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a responsive two-column flex container with the left navigation column configured as sticky or fixed relative to the viewport, and the right content column scrollable in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned in the left column starting at the very top-left of the viewport, this component occupies roughly 18–22% of the viewport width with a fixed min/max width. The ornate green plaque logo sits at the top, centered within this column with generous padding (24–32px) around it. Directly below, navigation links are arranged in a clean vertical stack, each on a single line, all-caps, equal typographic weight, and evenly spaced with about 12–16px vertical separation. The 'OUR RIDES' item includes a subtle down-caret positioned just to its right, aligned with the text baseline. The nav items occupy roughly the upper third to half of the column height; the remaining vertical space below is intentionally left empty to maintain the airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky or fixed left rail: remains pinned to the left edge and top of the viewport while the main content on the right scrolls, giving a stable navigation anchor.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Located in the right content column, this search form sits near the top, just below a thin horizontal rule that runs across the full width of the content area. It is aligned to the left edge of the right column, starting with a 24–32px vertical gap below the rule. The search input (and any accompanying button) spans about 60–70% of the right column width, leaving open space to its right to preserve the lightness of the layout. There is 16–24px of clear vertical space above and below the search bar to separate it visually from both the rule and the search summary below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; it scrolls with the rest of the right column content and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component appears directly beneath the search bar, separated by about 8–12px of vertical space. It forms a slim horizontal information band aligned with the left edge of the search bar and spanning roughly the same width (60–70% of the right column). The main text (e.g., result count and current query) is left-aligned, while optional controls like sort or time-range selectors can sit to the right within the same line, using subtle text or light underlined links to maintain the minimal aesthetic. The band is visually light—no heavy borders, just typography and perhaps subtle spacing to distinguish it from the background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a narrow strip in the vertical stack between the search bar and the filters/results area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned in the upper-left section of the main search results zone, this filter sidebar begins approximately 24–32px below the search summary. It hugs the left edge of the right content column, occupying around 22–26% of that column’s width. The filters are arranged in a simple vertical list, each filter group separated by 16–24px of vertical space and each individual filter option given around 8–12px vertical padding. Typography is understated and left-aligned, with no dense borders or panels—just text and light indicators (e.g., checkboxes or small bullets). The sidebar extends downward only as far as needed by its content, leaving blank vertical space if filters are short, reinforcing the minimal look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static by default but may be configured as sticky within the right column (sticking a little below the horizontal rule) so that filters remain visible as the results list scrolls, while still respecting the overall scroll of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This list occupies the remaining horizontal space to the right of the category filter sidebar in the main content area. It starts aligned to the same top baseline as the filter sidebar (about 24–32px below the search summary) and stretches to the right edge of the content column, giving it roughly 74–78% of the right column width. Results are laid out as a single vertical stack of cards, each full-width within this results column. There is a consistent 24–32px vertical gap between items, allowing the white background to show generously between them. The list flows downward, forming the primary scrolling content of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack; it scrolls within the right-hand column, with no fixed positioning. The list’s bottom edge is followed by the pagination controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each result item is a wide, low-density card that spans the full width of the results list column. It uses 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. The structure is predominantly vertical: the article title sits at the top-left, set in a slightly larger font, followed by a short line of metadata (date, category) and a 2–3 line excerpt beneath, each with 8–12px vertical spacing. On the right side of the top row, aligned with the title’s baseline, small, unobtrusive icon or text buttons for adding to the reading list and marking as favorite are placed, leaving generous space around them so they don’t crowd the title. The card has no heavy borders; any separation from the background is achieved via spacing and possibly a very subtle shadow or hairline rule at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical stack of results. Each card maintains consistent padding and spacing regardless of screen size, shrinking gracefully but preserving the vertical hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit below the last search result item, separated by 32–40px of vertical space to give a clear sense of the list ending. They are horizontally centered within the right content column, occupying roughly the middle 40–60% of the width, with plenty of white space to the left and right. Pagination elements (previous/next, page numbers) are spaced apart by 8–16px, using understated typography or minimal buttons that align with the overall editorial feel—no large, heavy UI components. The area is a low, flat strip of controls with ample breathing room above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the results list in the normal document flow; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves away from the bottom of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component forms the left half of a simple footer band that spans the full width of the page beneath both the navigation rail and the right content column. The footer begins after a generous 48–64px vertical gap below the pagination area, emphasizing its separation from the main content. Within the footer band (which has 24–32px vertical padding), the navigation/about content is left-aligned, starting at the same left margin as the right content column (not under the nav rail, to keep alignment consistent). It might contain a brief about paragraph and a small row or column of simple text links, each separated by 8–12px. The text is smaller and more muted than body text, reinforcing its tertiary importance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the page content; not fixed to the viewport. It expands downward as needed if the about or link content grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Sharing the same footer band as the footer navigation/about, this component occupies the right half of the footer area. It is right-aligned within the main content width, starting roughly opposite the about/links block, with 24–32px padding matching the footer’s vertical rhythm.
