The form is compact: a single-line email input and a subtle submit button arranged horizontally on larger screens, together spanning about 50–60% of the footer’s width on the right side, leaving empty space around them. On smaller screens, the input and button can stack vertically while maintaining generous spacing (8–12px between them and 16–24px above and below) to preserve the airy look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the footer band, flowing alongside the footer-navigation-and-about component in a simple two-column footer layout that collapses gracefully on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each search result item, this controller appears as a small, minimalist icon or text label (e.g., a bookmark symbol) located in the upper-right region of the card. It aligns horizontally with the title and sits either to the left or right of the favorites toggle, with 8–12px horizontal spacing between the two controls. The hit area is slightly larger than the icon itself (e.g., 24–32px square) but visually understated to avoid clutter. There is ample margin (at least 12–16px) between this control group and the card’s right edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the layout of each result card. It does not affect the card’s flow; it simply occupies the top-right corner of the card’s internal content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Also placed within each search result item, this controller appears as a subtle favorite indicator (e.g., an outlined heart). It sits in the top-right of the result card, adjacent to the reading list toggle, with 8–12px horizontal separation. Both controls form a small action cluster that aligns with the title’s baseline. Visual styling is light (thin line icons or small text labels) to maintain the editorial, low-UI feel while still being clearly tappable/clickable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the result item’s top row. It shares the same spatial behavior as the reading-list toggle, remaining anchored in the card’s top-right corner as the card resizes.

name: About
title: About
description: The About page loads descriptive content about the horse, the author, and the blog’s purpose via an API, and provides contextual navigation to core sections plus a newsletter subscription form.

core_features: Load and display About page content (title, body, optional image, last-updated metadata) using the getAboutContent interface.
Render in-body links (included in the loaded HTML content) that navigate to the homepage, article category listings, the contact page, and the photo gallery via URL-based navigation.
Expose global navigation options (header links and search) to move to other pages such as gallery.html, reading_list.html, favorites.html, and search_results.html using URL parameters.
Provide a footer newsletter subscription form that creates a NewsletterSubscription via the createNewsletterSubscription interface, with topic preferences, frequency, and email format.
user_workflows:
Load and read About content:
1. User navigates to about.html (via footer About link or direct URL).
2. On initial page load, the page shell triggers getAboutContent with no parameters.
3. The response is used to populate the document/page title from response.title.
4. The main content area renders response.body_html as HTML, including any internal links back to training, health, competitions, gear categories, the contact page, and the gallery.
5. If response.hero_image_url is present, an image component near the top of the content binds to that URL; if not present, the image area remains hidden.
6. If response.last_updated is present, a small metadata element displays it to indicate when the About information was last updated.

Navigate from About to category-specific blog posts:
1. While reading the About content, the user clicks a contextual link (contained within body_html) that refers to a specific post category (e.g., training, health, competitions, gear).
2. The link’s href points to articles.html with a categoryId query parameter derived from the link definition (e.g., articles.html?categoryId=training).
3. The browser navigates to articles.html, where that page uses categoryId from the URL to filter articles. The About page is not responsible for loading articles; it only constructs or renders the correct URL.

Navigate from About to Contact or Gallery:
1. Within the rendered body_html, the user clicks a link labeled for contacting the author.
2. The link points to contact.html with no additional parameters, and the browser navigates there.
3. Alternatively, the user clicks a link or header item that points to gallery.html.
4. The browser navigates to gallery.html; any gallery filtering happens on that page.

Use header search from the About page:
1. The user types a search term into the header search input.
2. Optionally, the user chooses a category filter in the header (if implemented as a select or separate control tied to the search form).
3. On submit, the search form composes a GET URL to search_results.html with query, category (if specified), page, and pageSize query parameters, setting page to 1 and pageSize to a value that the backend recognizes as a default (e.g., page=1&pageSize=default).
4. The browser navigates to search_results.html with those parameters; search_results.html is responsible for running the search and displaying Article results.

Subscribe to the newsletter from the footer:
1. The user scrolls to the footer and locates the newsletter subscription form.
2. The user enters their name and email into required fields.
3. The user selects or confirms topic preferences corresponding to wants_training_tips, wants_competition_updates, and wants_merchandise_offers (each as a checkbox or equivalent control).
4. The user selects a frequency value from the allowed enum (daily, weekly, monthly); the UI may default to one of these values such as weekly.
5. The user selects an email_format (html or plain_text); the UI may default to html.
6. On form submit, the component validates that required fields are provided and that email has a valid format. If validation passes, it calls createNewsletterSubscription with the current field values.
7. When createNewsletterSubscription returns, if response.success is true, the form displays response.message in a success state (e.g., inline text) and may optionally disable or reset the form. If response.success is false, the form displays response.message in an error state and leaves the current field values intact so the user can correct them.
8. No subscription data is stored in URL parameters; all subscription details are sent only through createNewsletterSubscription.
interactions: Initial data load: On page initialization, the About content component calls getAboutContent (no parameters). While waiting for a response, it can show a generic loading state. If the call fails, an error state is displayed with an option to retry the call.

Click navigation links in About content: The rendered body_html may contain anchor elements with href values pointing to index.html, articles.html with categoryId, contact.html, and gallery.html. Clicking these anchors triggers standard browser navigation using the specified URLs and query parameters.
Header navigation clicks: Header links for gallery.html, reading_list.html, favorites.html, and index.html navigate directly to those pages without additional API calls from this page.

Header search submission: The user submits the header search form. The form prevents submission if the query field is empty (client-side validation). If valid, it builds a URL search_results.html?query={encodedQuery}&category={encodedCategoryOrEmpty}&page=1&pageSize=default and triggers a full-page navigation.

Newsletter form input changes: As the user types in name and email or toggles topic preferences, the component updates its local form state. These changes are not written to any persistent store or URL; they are only used when calling createNewsletterSubscription.

Newsletter form submission: On submit, client-side validation ensures required fields (name, email, at least one topic preference if required by business rules, frequency, email_format) are present. On success, createNewsletterSubscription is called with the form state. Handlers for success and error update the UI with response.message and, if appropriate, reset or lock the form.

Error handling: If getAboutContent fails, the component shows an error message and a retry control. If createNewsletterSubscription fails (network or API-level error or response.success is false), the form displays a generic or API-provided error message, and the user can submit again without losing entered data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page itself does not require any URL parameters to determine what content to load; getAboutContent is always called without parameters. Page state is therefore stateless with respect to the about.html URL. Navigation to other pages from About uses URL parameters to control behavior on the destination pages:
- When the user clicks contextual category links inside the About content, the anchors point to articles.html?categoryId={categoryValue}, where categoryValue is one of the allowed Article.category enum values (training, health, competitions, gear). The About page does not validate or interpret categoryId; the Articles page is responsible for handling it.
- When the user submits the header search form, the browser navigates to search_results.html with a query string such as ?query={encodedQuery}&category={encodedCategoryOrEmpty}&page=1&pageSize=default. The About page always sets page to 1 on new searches and uses a fixed symbolic pageSize value (default) so that the search results page can interpret it according to its own logic.
- When the user clicks navigation items for reading_list.html or favorites.html, no additional parameters are added; those pages read their own data based on internal rules.
All other state (loading status of getAboutContent, current search input value before submission, newsletter form contents, success/error flags) is held in in-memory component state only and is not reflected in the URL or localStorage.

components.functionality: Top-level component that initializes the About page, orchestrates loading of About content, and composes header, main content, and footer (including the newsletter subscription form). It triggers the initial getAboutContent call on mount and passes the resulting data and loading/error states to child components.

Calls getAboutContent to retrieve About page data and renders the title, optional image, main HTML body, and last-updated information. It exposes loading and error states and ensures that in-body links work as navigation triggers to other pages.
Synchronizes the browser or document title with the title property returned by getAboutContent so that the About page’s title reflects the dynamic content from the API.
Displays the top-of-page image if hero_image_url is provided by getAboutContent. If hero_image_url is missing or empty, the component stays hidden without reserving space.
Shows the last_updated value from getAboutContent if present, typically as a small metadata text element. If last_updated is absent, it renders nothing.

Provides global navigation links from the About page to other key pages, including links to index.html, gallery.html, reading_list.html, favorites.html, and about.html (current page). It does not call any APIs; it only performs URL-based navigation on link click.

Captures a search query (and optional category) from the user while on the About page and, upon submission, navigates to search_results.html with query, category, page, and pageSize URL parameters. It performs client-side validation so that an empty query cannot be submitted.

Renders the body_html string from getAboutContent directly into the main content area, enabling HTML-defined headings, paragraphs, and inline links. The component ensures that links with hrefs pointing to index.html, articles.html, contact.html, and gallery.html use standard navigation and can be extended to track click events if needed.

Handles newsletter subscription from the footer. It manages form fields (name, email, wants_training_tips, wants_competition_updates, wants_merchandise_offers, frequency, email_format), validates them, and on submit calls createNewsletterSubscription. It then updates the UI based on the success boolean and message returned, and exposes the created NewsletterSubscription object if needed for confirmation.

Displays success or error feedback for the newsletter subscription process using the message property returned from createNewsletterSubscription. It subscribes to the result of the latest subscription attempt and adjusts its visual state to indicate success or failure.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout rather than a visible multi-column grid: a narrow fixed-width left column for logo + navigation and a wide fluid content area on the right. Likely implemented with a simple CSS flex or float layout rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a two-region composition: a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar for logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, open right canvas for the About content. The left rail hosts the global header-navigation in a top-to-bottom stack, while the right side presents a thin, understated header band (with search) that sits above the main About article area, which includes title, meta, hero image, article body, and a newsletter form at the end.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a two-region composition: a narrow, fixed-width left sidebar for logo and vertical navigation, and a wide, open right canvas for the About content. The left rail hosts the global header-navigation in a top-to-bottom stack, while the right side presents a thin, understated header band (with search) that sits above the main About article area, which includes title, meta, hero image, article body, and a newsletter form at the end.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly 20% of the viewport width (minimum ~240px, maximum ~320px) to the left navigation column and the remaining ~80% to the main content on the right. Vertically, the navigation occupies the upper half of the sidebar, leaving generous empty space below. In the right column, a shallow header band at the very top (about 56–72px tall) holds the search and is separated from the article by a thin horizontal rule; the article content below uses a comfortable max text width (~640–720px) and generous vertical spacing between elements (32–48px between major blocks).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain very low density: single-column article content with generous white margins on the right and left of the text block, ample vertical breathing room between title, meta, hero image, body, and newsletter form. Navigation items are evenly spaced with vertical padding around 8–12px and extra gaps (16–24px) between logical groups. The newsletter form is compact and unobtrusive, with enough padding (16–24px inside the form) to feel like a calm editorial insert rather than a hard-sell module.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the (existing) logo at the top-left, then travels downward through the vertically stacked header-navigation links. From there, the thin horizontal rule across the top of the right pane leads the eye horizontally into the header band, where the search form sits on the far right as a subtle interactive element. The gaze then drops to the About page title at the upper-left of the main content area, moves downward through the last-updated meta, hero image, and main body text, and finally reaches the newsletter subscription form and its feedback message near the bottom. The flow is vertical within each column, with a gentle left-to-right bridge via the top rule and search field.

layout.overall_description: The About page is presented as a calm, spacious two-column layout with a narrow, fixed left sidebar and a wide, mostly white content canvas on the right. At the upper-left of the viewport, the site’s decorative logo anchors the page; directly beneath it, the header-navigation runs as a vertical list of evenly spaced, all-caps links, occupying roughly the upper half of the sidebar and leaving clear white space below. To the right, occupying about four-fifths of the screen, the main content area begins with a shallow header band: across its bottom edge runs a thin horizontal rule, and aligned to the top-right within this band is a minimalist header-search-form. Below this rule, the About article content is laid out in a single, left-aligned column: the About title appears first with generous top margin, followed by understated last-updated meta, then a moderately large hero image, and then the main body text, all sitting within a comfortable max-width column and surrounded by ample margins. Near the bottom of this text column, a simple newsletter-subscription-form appears as a lightly framed or subtly separated block, with its feedback message reserved just beneath it. The left sidebar remains visually steady as the user scrolls the right-hand content, emphasizing the editorial, reading-focused experience with generous whitespace throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-shell spans the full viewport, establishing a two-column layout. On the far left, it reserves a vertical sidebar that occupies approximately 18–22% of the viewport width (with a minimum width around 240px and a maximum around 320px). On the right, it allocates the remaining 78–82% of the viewport for the main content canvas. The shell positions the sidebar flush to the left edge and runs it the full height of the viewport, while the right content area starts immediately to the right of this sidebar and stretches to the right edge. The shell also defines a top header band in the right column (about 56–72px tall) with a thin horizontal rule at its bottom edge; below this band is the vertical stack of About-specific components (title, meta, hero, body, newsletter). Horizontal and vertical spacing follows the 8px base grid, with 32–48px between major sections in the right column to preserve an airy, editorial feel. On scroll, the right content column scrolls independently while the left sidebar remains visually fixed in place.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Serves as the main structural container; left sidebar is effectively fixed relative to the viewport while the right content area scrolls vertically. On narrower viewports, the sidebar may collapse above the content, but in the default layout it remains a persistent left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader lives within the right content column, covering only the main About content region below the top header band. When active, it overlays or replaces the About title, meta, hero, body, and newsletter area with loading indicators aligned to their eventual positions: a placeholder bar where the title will be (near the top-left of the content column), smaller bars under it for meta, a large rectangular skeleton occupying the hero image area, and multiple lines for the body text, all aligned to the same left margin and max-width as the final content. It leaves the left sidebar and the header band (including the search form and horizontal rule) fully visible, reinforcing that global navigation remains usable while content loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within the bounds of the right-hand content column, stacking in the same vertical order as the actual content and occupying only the article content area below the top horizontal rule.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-title-binding is placed in the right content column, directly beneath the top header band and its horizontal rule. It sits near the top-left of the main content area, aligned with a consistent left content margin (e.g., 48–64px from the right column’s left boundary, or visually flush with the main text column). There is generous vertical spacing above the title—approximately 32–40px from the horizontal rule—to create a distinct separation between the header band and the article. The title spans only as wide as needed, but it shares the same max-width as the main body text (around 640–720px) to keep the reading column consistent. Additional space of about 8–12px separates the title from the last-updated meta that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the vertical flow of the right content column; scrolls naturally with the article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-hero-image appears in the right content column below the title and last-updated meta. It is left-aligned with the same content margin as the title and body text, maintaining a clean, single-column alignment. Vertically, there is a moderate gap (24–32px) between the meta line and the top of the hero image to let the image breathe and mark a clear transition. The image occupies a substantial horizontal portion of the text column—typically spanning 70–100% of the content width up to a comfortable max-width—while preserving white margins on its right for an editorial feel. Beneath the hero image, another 24–32px gap separates it from the beginning of the about-body-html-renderer text block, keeping the layout light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive element within the vertical content stack; scales proportionally within the content column’s max-width while maintaining its left alignment and aspect ratio.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-last-updated-meta is positioned directly under the About title within the right content column, forming a tight cluster with the title near the top of the article. It is left-aligned with the title and set in a smaller, lighter typographic style so it visually recedes. There is a small vertical gap (around 8–12px) between the bottom of the title and the meta, and a larger gap (24–32px) between the meta and the hero image below, subtly grouping the title and meta as one unit while keeping the hero visually separate. The meta text doesn’t stretch full width; it remains within the same max-width column as the body copy, preserving the single-column rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the article’s vertical flow, scrolling with the rest of the content and maintaining its relative position just beneath the title.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation occupies the left sidebar column, starting just below the existing logo element at the top-left of the page. It is arranged as a vertical stack of navigation items, each on a single line, typically all caps with equal visual weight. Each item has vertical padding of roughly 8–12px above and below, and minimal horizontal padding so that the text aligns in a clean left column within the sidebar. The nav list begins about 24–32px below the logo to create a clear separation. If there are grouped items (e.g., primary vs. secondary links), additional vertical spacing (16–24px) can be used between groups, but overall the list fills roughly the upper half of the sidebar height, with generous empty white space below. Any caret indicators, such as for expandable sections (e.g., ‘OUR RIDES’), are positioned inline to the right of their corresponding label, keeping the column narrow and tidy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in the left sidebar column, remaining in place relative to the viewport while the right-hand content area scrolls. Navigation items are vertically stacked and do not scroll out of view in the default desktop layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form is placed in the top header band of the right content column. It is aligned to the far right, horizontally, with a margin of about 32–48px from the right edge of the viewport, and vertically centered within the header band (which is about 56–72px tall). The thin horizontal rule runs along the bottom edge of this band, visually separating the search from the article content below. The search input is moderate in width—around 30–40% of the right column width—but visually light, with subtle borders to avoid overpowering the minimalist design. There is ample empty space to its left, keeping the header band feeling mostly empty and reinforcing the content-first nature of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the static top header band of the right column; remains at the top of the page and scrolls out of view only when the entire page scrolls. It does not float over content but stays fixed relative to the top of the right-hand content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-body-html-renderer forms the main text body of the About page and is located in the right content column beneath the hero image. It is left-aligned with the title and hero, with a consistent left margin and a max-width of roughly 640–720px to optimize readability. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the hero image and the first paragraph of body text, giving a clear visual break. The text flows downward in a single column, respecting the generous line spacing and paragraph spacing consistent with the editorial feel (e.g., 16–24px between paragraphs). The right side of the body text leaves a comfortable margin to the edge of the viewport, reinforcing the low-density, content-first design. No sidebars or additional columns interrupt this text; it remains a pure, linear reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical content flow of the right column, scrolling naturally as the user reads; it expands vertically as needed but remains constrained to the predefined text width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-subscription-form appears in the right content column after the main body text, positioned near the lower portion of the page but still within the same left-aligned content column as the article. It is separated from the preceding body text by a generous vertical gap of about 32–48px so it reads as a distinct but related section. The form itself is contained within a subtle block—either a faint border or a soft background tint—with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides, following the component padding guidelines. It spans the same max-width as the body text, keeping alignment consistent, and is centered on that column rather than full-bleed across the entire right pane. Input fields and the submit button are stacked vertically with 8–16px between them, maintaining the calm, uncluttered aesthetic. The entire block sits above a comfortable margin of whitespace before the page’s footer or end, letting it feel like a gentle invitation rather than a crowded footer widget.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the end of the article flow within the right content column. It scrolls into view after the body content and expands vertically as form elements and messages require.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-feedback-message is positioned directly beneath the newsletter-subscription-form’s input/button cluster, within the same padded block. It aligns to the left edge of the form content, occupying the same text width as the form labels, and uses a smaller, unobtrusive type style so it does not visually compete with the primary content. A modest vertical gap (8–12px) separates it from the bottom of the form controls above, and it may reserve a small amount of vertical space even when empty to prevent layout shift. This message sits entirely within the vertical column of the newsletter section, keeping the layout tidy and predictable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the bounds of the newsletter form block. It does not affect the placement of other major components; instead, it expands or contracts within the reserved feedback area as needed while the overall form position in the page layout remains fixed.
